Description: Display name of the model shown in ThoughtSpot search and Spotter.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Top-level semantic identifier; agents use the name to select the correct domain model without ambiguity in multi-model environments.
Human Importance: High — first label users see in the search bar and explorer
Agentic Importance: High — LLMs use the model name to route queries; wrong name causes model misselection
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Required; consumed by Cortex Analyst as the primary label. Con: Must be a valid Snowflake identifier — spaces require snake_casing.
Databricks UC Equivalent: View name in CREATE VIEW DDL
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Fully qualified catalog.schema.view_name gives agents a governance-aware locator. Con: Renaming requires DROP/CREATE.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: semantic_model.name
Other Platform Notes: LookML: explore label; Power BI: model name; AtScale: cube name

Description: Free-text description of the model's business purpose.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Human-readable context agents and LLMs use to decide which model to query; prevents model misselection in multi-model environments.
Human Importance: High — shown as tooltip and in Explorer; critical for human comprehension
Agentic Importance: High — primary signal for LLM model disambiguation; richer = more accurate routing
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Consumed by Cortex Analyst for model-level context. Con: No structured tagging — all context is free-text.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Consumed by Genie for natural-language understanding. Con: Single flat string; no structured sub-fields.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: semantic_model.description
Other Platform Notes: LookML: explore description; Atlan: userDescription + README; Power BI: model description

Description: List of ThoughtSpot table objects (and GUIDs) included in the model.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Defines the data perimeter of the semantic model; agents need to know which physical tables are reachable.
Human Importance: Low — internal plumbing; humans see columns not table lists
Agentic Importance: Medium — defines which physical tables an agent can query; a misconfigured perimeter causes cross-domain leakage
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Explicit physical FQN (DB.SCHEMA.TABLE) — more portable than GUIDs. Con: Multi-aliased tables (self-joins) require wrapper views.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Single source + join tree is easier for agents to traverse. Con: Multi-fact models need restructuring.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: semantic_model.model: ref('table')
Other Platform Notes: LookML: view sql_table_name; dbt: ref() model reference

Description: Inline join definitions specifying how tables connect (condition / type / cardinality).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Relationship graph is the backbone of semantic understanding; agents rely on join paths to resolve cross-table requests without raw SQL.
Human Importance: Low — humans do not see join definitions directly
Agentic Importance: High — join correctness directly determines whether agent-generated SQL produces correct results
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Named; discoverable by Cortex Analyst for automatic join resolution. Con: Equality-only joins on declared column pairs; no expression or inequality conditions.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Full SQL in on: — supports inequality and function-based conditions. Con: Agent must understand nested hierarchy to traverse.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: entities[] with type: foreign — MetricFlow resolves joins via entity graph
Other Platform Notes: LookML: join sql_on + relationship; AtScale: dimension-to-fact relationships

Description: Reference to a pre-defined join already declared in the ThoughtSpot Table TML.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Reuses centrally governed join definitions; agents benefit because join logic is defined once at the table layer — reducing inconsistency risk.
Human Importance: Low — internal reference; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Medium — inherited joins carry same semantics as inline; agent query correctness depends on correct resolution
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Same end result — resolved pair-wise relationships. Con: TS referencing_join indirection concept has no Snowflake analog.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Resolved joins become explicit SQL — portable and transparent. Con: TS indirection concept is lost.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt resolves via entity graph, not named join references
Other Platform Notes: LookML: explore join name; AtScale: relationship name

Description: Model-level augmentation joins — joins an uploaded CSV or external dataset to the model.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Supports ad-hoc data enrichment without modifying physical tables; agents can reach user-uploaded reference data through the same semantic interface.
Human Importance: Medium — enables self-service data blending for analysts
Agentic Importance: Low — uploaded datasets are unstructured; agents cannot reliably use ad-hoc uploads
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Augmentation table must be loaded into Snowflake and registered as a standard base_table.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: External datasets must be registered as UC tables before joining.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: LookML: derived tables for data blending

Description: UI folder structure organising columns into named panels in the ThoughtSpot search bar.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Improves navigability for humans; for agents folder names serve as implicit domain tags during schema introspection.
Human Importance: High — directly controls how humans browse and discover fields in the search panel
Agentic Importance: Low — agents query by name/synonym not by folder; minimal effect on query accuracy
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No grouping concept in Snowflake SV. Folder structure must be re-created in the BI or agent orchestration layer.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Genie has no panel/folder equivalent. Subject area metadata must be embedded in display_name or comment.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt Explorer organises by semantic_model
Other Platform Notes: LookML: group_label + group_item_label; Cube: views[].folders[]; Power BI: displayFolder

Description: Model-level pre-filters applied before every query (e.g. exclude test records; scope to active region).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Governance guardrail — ensures agents cannot accidentally query outside the permitted data scope.
Human Importance: Medium — users may be unaware filters exist; mandatory filters prevent accidental over-access
Agentic Importance: High — enforces data perimeter at query time; critical for compliant agent access
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not directly supported (no row-level pre-filter in SV YAML)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Must be a Snowflake row access policy or view wrapper. Pro: Row access policy is engine-enforced — stronger than an advisory filter.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native top-level filter in UC MV; simple conditions translate directly. Con: Complex OR logic or parameter-dependent filters require manual SQL rewriting.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter (per-metric Jinja filter)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: sql_always_where (invisible mandatory filter); Power BI: RLS DAX filter

Description: Flag that prevents users from removing a model-level filter.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures agents and users cannot bypass data access restrictions defined by the model owner; critical for compliance.
Human Importance: Low — hidden from users; compliance-facing
Agentic Importance: High — without mandatory enforcement agents can bypass scope restrictions
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake row access policies are always mandatory by design (stronger). No advisory flag needed.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported (filter: always enforced in UC MV)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: UC filter is always mandatory — no user-removable concept; stronger than a flag.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: LookML: sql_always_where is always mandatory; Power BI RLS always enforced

Description: Scopes a model filter to a specific table within a multi-fact model.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables per-fact-table data scoping; without this a filter can incorrectly restrict unrelated fact tables.
Human Importance: Low — internal; users do not configure this
Agentic Importance: Medium — misconfigured table scope causes incorrect data restriction on multi-fact agent queries
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No table-scoped filter. Must be a view wrapper on only the relevant table.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not directly supported (single filter: applies to full view)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Table-specific filtering requires separate metric views per fact or inline SELECT with WHERE as source.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter scoped per metric (closest equivalent)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: access_filter scoped to a join

Description: Rolling date window constraints limiting how far back ThoughtSpot scans on large fact tables.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Performance guardrail for agents; prevents full-table scans on large time-series datasets and communicates data freshness boundary.
Human Importance: Low — internal optimization; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Medium — prevents agents from triggering runaway queries on billions of historical rows
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No rolling window concept. Must be a WHERE clause in a view wrapper or enforced by a query rewriter.
Databricks UC Equivalent: filter: can express a fixed date predicate
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC filter is static SQL — not a dynamic rolling window. A rolling window requires a parameterized view or scheduled filter update.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt handles this via incremental model logic
Other Platform Notes: LookML: persist_for or PDT refresh_key

Description: Links pre-aggregated summary models to the base model for automatic query routing and performance optimization.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Transparent query acceleration; agents and users get fast responses at coarser granularities without knowing which physical table was queried.
Human Importance: Medium — users benefit from speed without being aware of the optimization
Agentic Importance: Low — agents do not need to know about routing; this is transparent infrastructure
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No query routing concept. Acceleration handled by materialized views or clustering keys outside the semantic layer.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native materialization in UC MV — can auto-materialize for query acceleration. Con: Less granular control than TS pre-aggregated models.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[].exports[].config.export_as: table (materialized output)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: PDTs (Persistent Derived Tables); AtScale: query routing to aggregate views

Description: Allows the model to bypass ThoughtSpot row-level security rules.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Controls whether an agent or service account sees filtered or unfiltered data; critical when building admin analytics tools.
Human Importance: Low — admin-facing only; regular users never set this
Agentic Importance: High — determines data visibility scope for service accounts and privileged agents
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No RLS bypass concept. Role-based access managed at Snowflake role/row access policy level.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Access entirely managed by Unity Catalog privileges and row filters. No model-level bypass hint.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: LookML: required_access_grants; Power BI: OLS (object-level security)

Description: Enables or disables ThoughtSpot's Spotter AI search for this model.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Top-level agent access switch; disabling prevents Spotter from querying the model. Equivalent to marking a semantic model as off-limits for AI.
Human Importance: High — determines whether the model appears in the AI search experience for users
Agentic Importance: High — binary on/off for all agent access to this model
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not supported (Cortex Analyst access controlled by Snowflake role grants)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No AI-enabled/disabled switch in SV YAML.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported (Genie access controlled by UC permissions and workspace sharing)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No YAML flag — access governed by UC privileges.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt Semantic Layer access controlled by API tokens and job permissions
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.public: false on a view (hides from API consumers)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: hidden: true on an explore; Atlan: certification status as soft gate

Description: Display name of the column shown in the ThoughtSpot search bar and Spotter.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Primary label agents use to reference data concepts; a clear business-friendly name eliminates ambiguity in natural-language queries.
Human Importance: High — most visible property; what users type in search and see in reports
Agentic Importance: High — primary identifier for NL query matching; poor names cause agent query misses
Snowflake SV Equivalent: name on each dimension / time_dimension / metric entry
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Used as the primary SQL identifier by Cortex Analyst. Con: Must be a valid Snowflake identifier — no spaces; display names need snake_casing.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: UC separates machine identifier (name) from human label (display_name) — best of both worlds. Con: More fields to maintain.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dimension.label / measure.label — display name separate from identifier name
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.title — display name separate from identifier
Other Platform Notes: LookML: label; Power BI: displayName; Cube: title

Description: Classifies a column as ATTRIBUTE (dimension) or MEASURE (metric).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Fundamental semantic classification; agents use this to know whether a field groups/filters data or aggregates it — the core distinction of any semantic layer.
Human Importance: Medium — implicit in the UX (measures aggregate; attributes filter)
Agentic Importance: High — without correct type classification agents misaggregrate or mis-filter data; most common source of wrong results
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Implicit — placement in dimensions[] vs time_dimensions[] vs metrics[]
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Structural placement is unambiguous — no parsing required. Con: No explicit column_type field; type inferred from array.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Implicit — placement in dimensions[] vs measures[]
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Same structural approach. Con: No time_dimensions[] in UC — all dates are plain dimensions.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dimension.type: categorical or time; measure.agg defines measure
Other Platform Notes: LookML: dimension type vs measure type; Power BI: ATTRIBUTE column vs measure

Description: Default aggregation function for a MEASURE column (SUM / COUNT / AVERAGE / MIN / MAX / COUNT_DISTINCT).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Governs how an agent aggregates a metric when no explicit aggregation is requested; prevents double-counting and incorrect roll-ups.
Human Importance: Medium — affects row totals and chart values users see
Agentic Importance: High — incorrect default aggregation produces silent wrong results at every agent query; one of the most impactful properties
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Aggregation is explicit in SQL expression — self-contained. Con: Changing aggregation requires editing the expr string.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Same SQL-expression approach; fully explicit. Con: No separate aggregation field to update.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measure.agg: sum | count | avg | min | max | count_distinct | median | percentile
Other Platform Notes: LookML: measure type; Power BI: measure DAX expression; AtScale: measure aggregation

Description: Hides a column from the ThoughtSpot search bar while keeping it available for formulas and joins.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Reduces noise for agents and users by hiding technical columns (FK keys; RLS anchors) that should not be queried directly.
Human Importance: High — directly controls what users see in the search panel; hiding FK clutter improves discoverability
Agentic Importance: High — agents should not attempt to query raw FK columns; hidden columns reduce mis-use risk
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All dimensions/metrics in SV are visible to Cortex Analyst. Omit FK columns from the YAML entirely.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All dimensions/measures in UC MV are exposed to Genie. Omit unwanted columns from the YAML.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not directly supported — omit from semantic_model to hide
Other Platform Notes: LookML: hidden: yes; Power BI: isHidden; Cube: public: false

Description: Marks a column as additive — explicitly allows summation across time for semi-additive models.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures agents compute totals correctly on metrics like inventory balance or headcount that should not be summed across time periods.
Human Importance: Low — internal flag; users do not see it directly
Agentic Importance: High — without this flag agents may sum snapshot metrics (e.g. headcount) across time producing nonsensical totals
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No is_additive flag. Semi-additive behavior must be encoded in metrics[].expr using NON ADDITIVE BY / window SQL.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native semi-additive support via window: config on measures — cleaner and more explicit than a flag.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measure.non_additive_dimension.window_choice: min or max
Other Platform Notes: LookML: no native semi-additive; custom SQL workaround; AtScale: semi-additive as first-class measure subtype; Power BI: semi-additive measure patterns via DAX

Description: Display format string for numbers and dates (e.g. #,##0.00 for currency; dd/MMM/yyyy for dates).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Controls how metric values are presented in natural-language responses; consistent formatting prevents agents from displaying raw floats where currency symbols are expected.
Human Importance: High — directly affects how values are displayed in reports and answers
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents benefit from format hints to present values correctly; incorrect format reduces response quality
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No format property in SV YAML. Apply in the BI tool or via TO_CHAR() in a Snowflake view wrapper.
Databricks UC Equivalent: format: with type: number / currency / percentage and sub-properties (v1.1)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: UC has a structured format: object with typed options including decimal_places and abbreviation. Con: Coarser than TS arbitrary format strings; custom date formats have no UC equivalent.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported — formatting is BI-layer concern in dbt
Other Platform Notes: LookML: value_format or value_format_name; Power BI: formatString; Cube: format

Description: ISO currency code (e.g. USD / EUR) that adds a currency symbol and formatting to a measure.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures agents and BI tools render monetary values with the correct currency symbol; prevents region ambiguity in multi-currency models.
Human Importance: High — users expect currency symbols on monetary values; absence causes confusion
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents benefit from currency hints to present monetary answers correctly
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No iso_code field in SV YAML. Embed currency symbol in description or handle in the BI layer.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native currency format support in UC MV including currency_code. Pro: Controls both symbol and decimal formatting.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported — BI-layer concern
Other Platform Notes: LookML: value_format_name: usd; Power BI: formatString with currency

Description: Assigns a geographic role to a column: latitude / longitude / state / zip / city / country.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables spatial queries and map visualizations; agents with geospatial reasoning can use geo_config to identify geographic columns.
Human Importance: High — enables map chart visualizations in ThoughtSpot UI; without it geo columns render as plain text
Agentic Importance: Low — most agents do not generate map queries; geo roles are rarely consumed programmatically
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No geospatial column classification in SV YAML. Migrate as plain dimension with description noting the geo role.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No geospatial column metadata in UC MV; Genie has no map visualization driven by semantic layer geo roles.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: dataCategory (Country, City, PostalCode, Longitude, Latitude) — strong geo typing for map visuals

Description: Excludes a column from SpotIQ's automated AI-driven analysis (e.g. suppress raw lat/long or internal IDs).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Prevents agents from generating nonsensical automated insights on technical columns; controls signal quality of AI-generated analyses.
Human Importance: Medium — determines which columns appear in auto-generated SpotIQ insights; reduces noise
Agentic Importance: Low — SpotIQ is a ThoughtSpot-specific feature with no cross-platform equivalent
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Cortex Analyst has no SpotIQ-equivalent; exclusion is moot.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Genie has no auto-analysis feature equivalent to SpotIQ.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot-specific; no cross-platform equivalent

Description: Flags a column as preferred in Search IQ / natural-language query suggestions.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Guides agents toward the most relevant fields when multiple semantically similar columns exist; improves precision of AI-generated queries.
Human Importance: High — preferred fields surface first in search suggestions; improves human discoverability
Agentic Importance: High — preference signal helps agents select the most relevant column when multiple similar options exist
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Cortex Analyst relies on descriptions and synonyms for relevance ranking; no explicit preferred-field hint.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Genie uses display_name, synonyms, and comment for relevance — no explicit preferred flag.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported — synonyms and descriptions carry this signal
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not supported — public: true (default) + descriptions carry this signal
Other Platform Notes: LookML: no explicit preferred flag; Lightdash: tags for preferred columns

Description: Free-text AI directive or description for Spotter — explains the column's business meaning and query caveats (e.g. 'Exclude nulls from averages for this metric').
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: The most important agentic property in ThoughtSpot. Provides the LLM with business context, calculation caveats, and query guidance that cannot be inferred from column names or aggregation type.
Human Importance: Low — not visible to users; internal AI directive
Agentic Importance: High — critical for accurate agent responses; the difference between correct metric interpretation and a plausible but wrong answer
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Merged into description (no separate ai_context field)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Semantic specificity is lost — Cortex Analyst treats this as documentation not an instruction. Con: Must be rewritten from directive to documentation form.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Merged into comment (no separate ai_context field)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Same limitation — UC has no per-column AI instruction field. Genie reads comment as context but cannot distinguish description from instruction.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: semantic_model.description + measure.description + dimension.description — all consumed by dbt Copilot and MCP server
Other Platform Notes: Snowflake: AI_SQL_GENERATION (view-level AI instruction); Atlan: README per asset (richest context); OpenMetadata: per-column description with vector embedding

Description: Explicit sort order array for attribute dimension values (e.g. [USA, UK, France]).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Governs how agents sort categorical dimension values in responses; prevents alphabetical ordering from misrepresenting priority or business hierarchy.
Human Importance: High — category sort order is frequently important for human interpretation of charts and tables
Agentic Importance: Low — agents typically apply query-specified sort; custom_order is a display hint not a query constraint
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No custom sort in SV YAML. Apply in the BI tool or via a Snowflake view wrapper with ORDER BY.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No custom sort in UC MV. Apply via CASE expression in the dimension's expr or in the Genie dashboard layer.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported
Other Platform Notes: LookML: order: asc/desc on dimension; Power BI: sortByColumn — maps one column's sort to another column's values

Description: Default time granularity for date columns (DAILY / WEEKLY / MONTHLY / QUARTERLY / YEARLY / AUTO).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Tells agents the intended analysis granularity for a date dimension; without this agents may over-specify granularity leading to performance issues.
Human Importance: High — controls the default time grain in charts and queries; matches user expectations
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents benefit from a grain hint to avoid unnecessarily fine-grained queries on large time-series tables
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV has no default granularity hint on time_dimensions. Agents choose granularity from query context.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC has no default granularity on date dimensions. Agents should infer from column name or comment.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dimension.type_params.time_granularity defines the minimum grain (not default query grain)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: dimension_group timeframes list defines available grains; LookML does not set a single default

Description: Alternative names recognized as equivalent to the column name (e.g. 'Revenue' also matches 'Sales' / 'Turnover').
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Critical for NLP robustness; agents often receive business vocabulary that differs from technical column names. Synonyms expand the vocabulary agents can use to reach the correct column.
Human Importance: High — natural search terms users type that do not match the technical column name; dramatically improves search hit rate
Agentic Importance: High — without synonyms agents frequently fail to resolve the correct column when users phrase requests in business terms
Snowflake SV Equivalent: synonyms[] on each dimension / time_dimension / metric entry
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Directly consumed by Cortex Analyst for NL matching. Con: No synonym weighting — all synonyms are equal.
Databricks UC Equivalent: synonyms[] on each dimension and measure (up to 10; max 255 chars each — v1.1)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Consumed by Genie for NL understanding. Con: Up to 10 synonyms per field; no weighting.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dimension.label + multiple labels can be used for synonym coverage
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not natively supported — use title as primary alias
Other Platform Notes: LookML: no native synonyms; Power BI: Q&A synonyms per table and column with linguistic schema; ThoughtSpot synonyms are the strongest query-time equivalent

Description: Assigns the column to one or more named UI folders in the ThoughtSpot data panel.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Improves human navigability; for agents that introspect model structure folder names provide implicit domain classification.
Human Importance: High — controls how humans browse fields; good folder structure reduces time-to-insight
Agentic Importance: Low — agents query by name/synonym not by folder location; minimal effect on query accuracy
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No grouping/folder concept in SV YAML. Agent introspection must rely on column names and descriptions alone.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No folder/panel structure in UC MV. Genie organises columns by dimension/measure type only.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dbt Explorer groups by semantic_model — no column-level folders
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.views[].folders[] — named folder groups in views
Other Platform Notes: LookML: group_label; Cube: views[].folders[]; Power BI: displayFolder per column

Description: Controls ThoughtSpot text-search indexing: full indexing (default) / PREFIX_ONLY / DONT_INDEX.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Search performance tuning; suppressing indexing on high-cardinality columns prevents ThoughtSpot search from bloating. No effect on agent SQL generation.
Human Importance: Medium — affects search autocomplete performance on high-cardinality string columns
Agentic Importance: Low — indexing is a ThoughtSpot-specific search hint; irrelevant to external agents generating SQL
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Dropped silently. Cortex Analyst does not use text-search indexes.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Dropped silently. Databricks Genie resolves columns from YAML metadata not text indexes.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot-specific feature with no cross-platform equivalent

Description: Unique identifier for a formula used to reference it from columns[].formula_id.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Internal reference key; ensures formulas are correctly linked to their column definitions. Agents do not consume this directly but correctness is a prerequisite for all formula-based metrics.
Human Importance: Low — internal reference; never visible to users
Agentic Importance: Low — an implementation detail that enables formula-column linking; agents interact with the result not the ID
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not applicable — SV embeds expressions directly in metrics[].expr
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Not applicable — UC MV embeds expressions directly in measures[].expr
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt uses measure.name as the reference identifier
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Not applicable
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: No equivalent in any platform; other platforms reference formulas by name

Description: Display name of the formula — becomes the column display name in ThoughtSpot.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Human and agent-facing label for computed metrics. A descriptive formula name (e.g. 'Net Promoter Score' not 'NPS_CALC') improves agent query accuracy.
Human Importance: High — the label users see for calculated metrics in search and reports
Agentic Importance: High — formula names are the primary labels agents use to identify computed metrics; poor names cause query misses
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Column name and formula name are unified in SV. Con: Must be a valid Snowflake identifier.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: UC allows name (machine identifier) and display_name (human label) to differ. Con: More fields to maintain.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric.name + metric.label (display name separate from identifier)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: measure label; Power BI: measure name + description

Description: ThoughtSpot formula expression using ThoughtSpot's formula language (sum, count_distinct, group_aggregate, moving_average, etc.).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: The business logic layer of the semantic model. Agents depend on correct formula expressions to compute governed metrics consistently across all queries.
Human Importance: Low — formula syntax is technical; users see the result not the expression
Agentic Importance: High — formula expression correctness determines whether agents compute metrics correctly; a wrong expression produces plausible but incorrect results
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native Snowflake SQL is portable and transparent to any SQL tool. Con: ThoughtSpot-specific functions require translation; some cannot translate.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native Databricks SQL (Spark SQL). Pro: Richer composition via MEASURE() references and FILTER (WHERE ...). Con: Same translation burden from TS formula language.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measure.expr (SQL expression over the underlying dbt model)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: measure sql; Power BI: measure DAX expression; AtScale: calculated member expression

Description: Display name of a runtime parameter (e.g. 'Fiscal Year' / 'Region Filter').
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Runtime context injection — allows agents to set analysis parameters dynamically (date ranges; scenario selectors) rather than requiring separate model versions per scenario.
Human Importance: High — users see and interact with parameters in the TS UI as dropdowns and sliders
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents can inject parameter values at query time enabling dynamic scenario analysis without separate models
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No runtime parameter equivalent in SV YAML. All parameter-referencing formulas are omitted.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no runtime parameter concept. No equivalent to ThoughtSpot's user-settable runtime variables.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter uses Jinja templating for dynamic conditions (closest equivalent)
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not supported natively; Cube.dev BYOLLM can inject context via meta
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: slicers and calculation groups provide dynamic parameter-like behavior; LookML: user_attribute-driven dynamic SQL (Liquid templating)

Description: Data type of the parameter (INT64 / DOUBLE / CHAR / DATE / BOOL).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Defines what kind of value the agent can inject at query time; type safety prevents agents from passing dates where integers are expected.
Human Importance: Low — data type validation is handled by the TS UI; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Medium — type safety prevents agents from injecting invalid values that cause silent query errors
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in SV YAML.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: No cross-platform equivalent for runtime parameter types

Description: Default value used when no parameter override is provided.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures agents get a sensible result when no parameter is explicitly set; prevents null-driven query failures on parameterized formulas.
Human Importance: Medium — determines the default view users see before adjusting parameters
Agentic Importance: Medium — prevents agents from querying parameterized formulas without a value and receiving null results
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in SV YAML.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: No cross-platform equivalent

Description: Restricts a parameter to a fixed list of allowed values.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Prevents agents from injecting invalid values; enables parameter values to surface in UI pickers and AI suggestions.
Human Importance: High — provides dropdown UI for users; eliminates free-text input errors
Agentic Importance: Low — agents can derive valid values from list but most platforms lack this concept entirely
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in SV YAML.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: slicer with specified values; LookML: parameter with allowed_values

Description: Restricts a parameter to a numeric range (min / max / inclusive flags).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Validates that agent-supplied numeric parameters fall within a meaningful business range (e.g. discount between 0 and 100%).
Human Importance: Medium — provides slider UI with bounded range for users
Agentic Importance: Low — agents rarely check range metadata; most simply pass a value
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in SV YAML.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: No strong cross-platform equivalent; LookML allows parameter with type: number

Description: Join condition expression: [FACT::FK] = [DIM::PK] (ThoughtSpot bracket notation).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Defines the data relationships agents traverse when combining dimensions and metrics across tables; incorrectly defined joins produce silently wrong results.
Human Importance: Low — humans do not see or write join conditions
Agentic Importance: High — join condition correctness is the single most impactful factor in agent query accuracy across tables
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Simple equality join pair — easy to validate. Con: Only equality joins on declared columns; no expression or function-based conditions.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Full SQL in on: — supports functions, inequalities, compound conditions. Pro: More expressive than TS bracket notation.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Entity graph (MetricFlow resolves joins automatically via entity type matching)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: join sql_on; AtScale: relationship condition; Power BI: relationship from/to columns

Description: Join type: INNER / LEFT_OUTER / RIGHT_OUTER / OUTER.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Determines which rows are included when an agent queries across tables; wrong join type produces row count discrepancies.
Human Importance: Low — humans do not configure join types
Agentic Importance: High — INNER vs LEFT_OUTER directly changes row counts; a wrong join type causes systematic agent query errors that are hard to detect
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not supported (join type not declared in SV YAML)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Cortex Analyst infers join semantics from schema; inability to specify LEFT vs INNER means outer-join semantics for optional dimensions may be wrong.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Implicit — UC MV always uses LEFT OUTER JOIN for all referenced joins
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Consistent LEFT JOIN behavior — agents always see all primary fact rows. Con: No INNER join option for required relationships.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: MetricFlow uses LEFT JOIN by default for all entity joins
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.joins[].relationship determines join direction; all cube joins are LEFT JOINs
Other Platform Notes: LookML: join type (left_outer / full_outer / inner / cross); AtScale: relationship type

Description: Cardinality declaration: MANY_TO_ONE (most common for fact-to-dimension joins).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Helps ThoughtSpot's query engine avoid fan-out errors on incorrect join paths; an agent querying MANY_TO_ONE knows metrics are safe to aggregate.
Human Importance: Low — internal metadata; never seen by users
Agentic Importance: High — fan-out on wrong cardinality causes inflated metric values; agents need cardinality awareness to generate correct GROUP BY logic
Snowflake SV Equivalent: primary_key on right_table implicitly declares ONE side
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: primary_key declaration acts as cardinality hint for Cortex Analyst. Con: No explicit MANY_TO_ONE / ONE_TO_ONE declaration; cardinality is inferred.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not declared — UC infers cardinality from data and on: clause
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No cardinality metadata in UC MV. Agents receive no fan-out warning if a join is misconfigured.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: entity type: primary / foreign defines cardinality (MetricFlow enforces this)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: relationship type per join (many_to_one / one_to_many); AtScale: relationship cardinality

Description: Alternative name for a table when the same physical table appears more than once (self-join or role-playing dimension).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables role-playing dimension patterns (e.g. Order Date vs Ship Date from the same date table); agents can query both roles without join ambiguity.
Human Importance: Low — internal; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Medium — role-playing dimensions without aliases cause agent confusion when similar column names exist on the same physical table
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Multiple tables[] entries with distinct name: values (requires wrapper views for Cortex)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Each alias becomes an independent table entry. Con: Requires wrapper views to avoid Cortex Analyst column-name conflicts.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Multiple joins[] entries with distinct name: values
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Nested join tree naturally supports multiple aliases for the same physical table.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: entity.role disambiguates multiple instances of the same entity type
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not directly supported; use sql with aliased CTEs
Other Platform Notes: LookML: from: and alias: on a join to use the same view under different names

Description: The GUID of the ThoughtSpot table object — binds the model to physical tables.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Internal ThoughtSpot reference; not exposed to agents. Required for TML import/export fidelity; without correct GUIDs models fail to import.
Human Importance: Low — internal plumbing; never visible to users
Agentic Importance: Low — an implementation detail for TML management; agents interact with the resulting model not the GUID
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Human-readable FQN instead of opaque GUIDs. Pro: More portable and inspectable by agents.
Databricks UC Equivalent: source: and joins[].source use fully-qualified catalog.schema.table
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Same human-readable FQN. Pro: Agents can read data lineage directly from the YAML.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: model: ref('dbt_model_name') — human-readable model reference
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.sql_table or cube.sql — human-readable table reference
Other Platform Notes: All other platforms use human-readable table references; ThoughtSpot GUIDs are the exception

Description: Named reusable filter predicates defined on a table within the semantic view (YAML-only; no DDL equivalent).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Reusable named WHERE conditions that Cortex Analyst can apply to restrict specific table data; more modular than embedding filters in SQL expressions.
Human Importance: Low — internal; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: High — named filters can be referenced in verified queries and AI instructions; helps agent scope queries correctly
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Reusable named predicates — define once, reference in multiple contexts. Con: No DDL equivalent; YAML-only.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC's top-level filter: is a single global predicate; no named reusable filters.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter (per-metric Jinja filter — closest equivalent)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.segments[] — reusable named filter predicates; a stronger equivalent
Other Platform Notes: No direct equivalent in ThoughtSpot

Description: Boolean flag on a dimension telling Cortex Analyst that sample_values[] is the exhaustive set of possible values.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables Cortex Analyst to generate accurate IN-filter SQL using the known value set; prevents agents from guessing invalid dimension values.
Human Importance: Low — internal hint; users see the effect in accurate filter dropdowns
Agentic Importance: High — without is_enum agents may apply invalid filter values causing empty result sets or query errors
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Tells Cortex Analyst to treat sample_values as authoritative — dramatically improves literal filter accuracy.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in UC MV. Genie must infer valid values from data at query time.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: values-based Q&A synonyms provide similar grounding

Description: Array of representative dimension values shown to Cortex Analyst for grounding natural-language filter resolution.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Provides Cortex Analyst with known valid filter values; prevents agents from hallucinating non-existent dimension values in WHERE clauses.
Human Importance: High — sample values can surface as suggested filter options in the UI
Agentic Importance: High — grounding agents with known valid values is one of the most effective ways to prevent hallucinated filter values
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Directly consumed by Cortex Analyst to resolve literal filters. Pro: Combine with is_enum for exhaustive value sets. Con: Must be manually maintained as data evolves.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Genie must query live data at runtime to resolve filter values; no static sample value grounding.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt handles this via data contracts and tests
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not applicable — Cube AI value search provides dynamic value resolution
Other Platform Notes: Lightdash: no static sample values; Atlan: sampleDataUrl provides sample data but not queryable filter values

Description: Declares that a metric is semi-additive and specifies the dimension across which it cannot be summed (e.g. account_balance at end of period).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures Cortex Analyst generates LAST_VALUE or FIRST_VALUE patterns instead of SUM for snapshot metrics; prevents silent double-counting errors.
Human Importance: Low — internal semantic hint; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: High — semi-additive metrics (balances, headcount, inventory) are the most common source of silently wrong agent query results
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (non_additive_dimensions[] on metrics — YAML; NON ADDITIVE BY clause in DDL)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: First-class semi-additive declaration with sort direction and null order control. Pro: Cortex Analyst natively generates correct window SQL.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native semi-additive config in UC MV with first/last control. Pro: Cleaner than ThoughtSpot's is_additive flag.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measure.non_additive_dimension.window_choice: min | max
Other Platform Notes: AtScale SML: semi-additive as first-class measure subtype; Power BI: LASTDATE / FIRSTDATE DAX patterns

Description: Free-text instructions injected into the Cortex Analyst SQL generation prompt (e.g. 'always round revenue to 2 decimal places' / 'prefer INNER JOIN for order facts').
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: View-level AI instruction that shapes how Cortex Analyst generates SQL; more powerful than description because it directly modifies the LLM's generation behavior.
Human Importance: Low — internal AI directive; never shown to users
Agentic Importance: High — closest Snowflake equivalent to ThoughtSpot's ai_context at the view level; directly modifies agent SQL generation behavior
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (AI_SQL_GENERATION clause in CREATE SEMANTIC VIEW DDL)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Direct prompt injection into Cortex Analyst generation context. Con: No YAML equivalent; DDL-only. Con: Free-text — no structured schema for hints.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent view-level AI instruction in UC MV YAML. Descriptions and comments are passive context only.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: No equivalent in ThoughtSpot or Databricks; unique to Snowflake Cortex Analyst

Description: Free-text instructions controlling when Cortex Analyst should reject a question or request clarification.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables semantic guardrails — instructs the agent to decline out-of-scope questions or ask follow-up questions before generating SQL.
Human Importance: Low — internal AI directive; users see the effect as refusal or clarification prompts
Agentic Importance: High — prevents agents from attempting to answer questions outside the model's semantic scope; reduces hallucination risk on edge-case queries
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (AI_QUESTION_CATEGORIZATION clause in CREATE SEMANTIC VIEW DDL)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Declarative rejection/clarification behavior embedded in the semantic model itself. Con: DDL-only; no YAML equivalent.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in UC MV. Genie's question-handling behavior is controlled by workspace settings not the metric view definition.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: No equivalent in any other platform; unique Snowflake capability

Description: Example question-and-SQL pairs stamped with verified_at timestamp and verified_by contact; onboarding questions surface as suggested prompts.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Pre-validated Q&A pairs that directly ground Cortex Analyst's SQL generation; the most powerful way to improve agent accuracy on known common questions.
Human Importance: High — ONBOARDING_QUESTION examples surface as suggested prompts in the Cortex Analyst UI; directly guides human exploration
Agentic Importance: High — verified queries serve as few-shot examples that significantly improve agent SQL accuracy on similar questions
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (AI_VERIFIED_QUERIES in DDL; verified_queries[] in YAML — with name / question / sql / verified_at / verified_by / use_as_onboarding_question)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Verified examples directly influence Cortex Analyst generation. Pro: Onboarding questions surface as clickable prompts. Pro: Dec 2025 optimization auto-enriches the model from accumulated verified queries.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No verified query concept in UC MV. Genie learns from user feedback separately but this is not embedded in the metric view definition.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[] — pre-defined queries that can be materialized; closest dbt equivalent
Other Platform Notes: Lightdash: self-improving semantic layer updates descriptions from query feedback — closest conceptual equivalent

Description: Marks a fact or metric as private — excluded from user-facing SQL generation by Cortex Analyst but usable in derived metric expressions.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables intermediate calculation columns that agents can use as building blocks for derived metrics without exposing them to direct user queries.
Human Importance: Low — private fields are invisible to users by design
Agentic Importance: Medium — allows layered metric construction without polluting the query interface
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (PRIVATE | PUBLIC modifier on facts and metrics in DDL; access_modifier in YAML)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Clean separation between internal building blocks and user-facing metrics. Con: No equivalent in other platforms.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All measures in UC MV are queryable. Intermediate measures must be implemented differently (e.g. via MEASURE() composition).
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measures created with create_metric: false (measure exists but no public metric is auto-generated)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].public: false — hides measure from API consumers
Other Platform Notes: No direct equivalent in ThoughtSpot

Description: Pointer to a Snowflake Cortex Search Service enabling fuzzy/semantic literal search on dimension values at query time.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables Cortex Analyst to resolve fuzzy dimension values (e.g. 'appl' → 'Apple Inc.') using vector search rather than exact string matching.
Human Importance: Medium — improves filter accuracy for users who type partial or approximate dimension values
Agentic Importance: High — fuzzy value resolution is critical for agents handling natural-language filter requests; prevents empty results from minor spelling variations
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (WITH CORTEX SEARCH SERVICE clause on dimensions — DDL and YAML)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Direct integration with Snowflake's vector search for dimension value resolution. Pro: No external vector store needed.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Genie resolves filter values by scanning data at query time; no semantic search integration on dimension values.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt handles via data contracts
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.meta.ai.searchable: true — flags dimension for value-level search in Cube AI
Other Platform Notes: No direct ThoughtSpot equivalent; ThoughtSpot uses index_type for search optimization

Description: Cross-table metrics defined at the semantic view level (not on a specific table) — enable ratio and formula metrics that span multiple tables.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables governed cross-table metric compositions (e.g. revenue / customers) that agents can query without writing raw multi-table SQL.
Human Importance: Low — internal metric definition; users see the resulting metric
Agentic Importance: High — cross-table derived metrics are essential for ratio KPIs that agents need to compute correctly
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (METRICS clause at view level in DDL; September 2025 addition)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: First-class cross-table metrics in the semantic model. Con: No equivalent in the older YAML spec.
Databricks UC Equivalent: measures[].expr using MEASURE() references spans across all joined tables
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: MEASURE() composition naturally spans all join-connected tables; no separate view-level concept needed.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric type: derived — formula over other metrics spanning semantic models
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].type: number — computed expression referencing other measures
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: formula columns that reference columns from multiple tables achieve similar results

Description: Inline conditional aggregate applying a WHERE clause to only a specific measure (e.g. SUM(revenue) FILTER (WHERE status = 'complete')).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables governed conditional metrics without separate tables or views; agents can request conditional metrics clearly from the YAML definition.
Human Importance: Low — internal SQL expression; users see the resulting conditional metric
Agentic Importance: High — cleaner and more transparent than CASE WHEN; agents can read the filter condition from the YAML to understand what the metric represents
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV requires CASE WHEN inside the metrics[].expr. Less readable and harder for agents to interpret semantically.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: SQL standard syntax. Pro: Multiple filtered variants of the same base metric can coexist. Pro: Agents can discover filter conditions directly from the YAML.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter using Jinja syntax (per-metric filter applied via WHERE)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].filters[] — inline filter predicates on a measure
Other Platform Notes: LookML: measure filters[]; ThoughtSpot achieves this via if/then/else formulas

Description: References a named measure from within another measure's expr (e.g. MEASURE(revenue) / NULLIF(MEASURE(orders), 0)).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables governed metric composition — agents can build ratio metrics on top of atomic measures without replicating aggregation logic; creates a discoverable metric dependency graph.
Human Importance: Low — internal SQL pattern; users see the resulting derived metric
Agentic Importance: High — agents can introspect the dependency graph between measures from the YAML; promotes reuse over duplication
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV requires duplicating the SQL in a derived metrics[] expr; no cross-metric reference syntax.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Promotes reuse — base metrics defined once; derived metrics reference them symbolically. Con: MEASURE() references must point to measures in the same metric view.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric type: derived with input_metrics[] — closest dbt equivalent
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].type: number referencing other cube measures
Other Platform Notes: AtScale SML: calculated members that reference other measures; ThoughtSpot: formula columns referencing other formula columns

Description: Declarative time-series and semi-additive measure config: range (cumulative / trailing N / current), order (time dimension), semiadditive (first / last) — Experimental v1.1.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Native window measure semantics allow agents to request 'cumulative revenue' or 'end-of-month balance' without knowing the underlying window SQL; more readable than embedded OVER() clauses.
Human Importance: Low — internal config; users see the resulting cumulative or snapshot metric
Agentic Importance: High — declarative window config is more transparent to agents than raw OVER() SQL; semiadditive: last communicates snapshot semantics that agents need to aggregate correctly
Snowflake SV Equivalent: NON ADDITIVE BY (semiadditive DDL) or window SQL in metrics[].expr
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV requires OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...) SQL written directly in expr — less readable and harder for agents to interpret.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Declarative — agents and tools can read semiadditive: last and understand snapshot semantics without parsing SQL. Pro: Separate first/last options are more expressive than ThoughtSpot's is_additive flag.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric type: cumulative (window + grain_to_date); measure.non_additive_dimension for semi-additive
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: moving_average / cumulative_sum formulas; LookML: rolling_window measure type

Description: Query acceleration configuration: schedule, mode: relaxed, and materialized_views[] with aggregated or unaggregated types — embedded in the metric view definition.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Transparent query caching for agents; pre-materialized views enable sub-second responses on expensive metrics without agents being aware of the optimization.
Human Importance: Low — internal infrastructure optimization; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Low — agents benefit from faster responses but do not interact with materialization config directly
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not supported directly in SV YAML
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake provides materialized views and dynamic tables as separate objects external to the semantic view definition.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Native (materialization: at top level — Experimental v1.1)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Acceleration policy embedded in the semantic layer definition — single place to manage both semantics and performance.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[].exports[] — materialize metric output to a warehouse table
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.pre_aggregations[] — declarative rollup scheduling in the data model
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: aggregated_models for pre-aggregated routing; LookML: PDTs (Persistent Derived Tables)

Property: Databricks: display_name on dimensions and measures
Description: A separate human-readable label distinct from the machine-identifier name field — allows both clean SQL identifiers and readable UI labels simultaneously.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Solves the tension between SQL-valid identifiers (snake_case) and user-friendly labels; agents can use display_name for UI output while using name for SQL generation.
Human Importance: High — human-readable display names in Genie and AI/BI Dashboards without sacrificing identifier clarity
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents can use display_name in responses and name in SQL generation; reduces ambiguity when name and label differ significantly
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not supported — name serves as both identifier and label
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All name fields must satisfy Snowflake identifier rules and serve as both SQL identifier and display label; forces a compromise.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Native (display_name: on dimensions and measures — v1.1)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Clean separation between machine name and human label. Con: More fields to maintain per column.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dimension.label + measure.label — display name always separate from identifier
Other Platform Notes: LookML: label on all fields; Power BI: displayName; Cube: title — all three separate identifier from label better than Snowflake SV

Description: Array of shared column names used as the join condition (alternative to on: SQL expression) — e.g. using: [customer_id].
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Simpler join syntax when both tables share identically-named key columns; reduces join definition verbosity for natural key joins.
Human Importance: Low — internal plumbing; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Low — minimal impact on agent behavior; agents interact with join results not join syntax
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not supported (relationships always use left_column / right_column pair)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV always requires explicit column naming in relationship_columns.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Native (using: on joins — alternative to on:)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: More concise for natural key joins. Con: Columns must have identical names on both sides; not applicable for renamed FK columns.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — MetricFlow uses entity graph not explicit SQL join conditions
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not applicable — Cube uses full SQL ON clause
Other Platform Notes: No equivalent in ThoughtSpot; minor convenience feature

Description: Explicit declaration of join key columns with semantic entity types — MetricFlow uses the entity graph to automatically resolve all joins between semantic models.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Entity-based join resolution means no manual join conditions; MetricFlow infers the correct join path from entity type declarations — more semantic and less error-prone than explicit SQL join conditions.
Human Importance: Low — internal join resolution; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: High — entity types make join logic semantically explicit and machine-discoverable; agents querying via the dbt MCP server never need to know the underlying join conditions
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not applicable — Snowflake SV uses explicit relationship_columns
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Explicit FK column pairs. Con: More verbose than entity-based automatic join resolution.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not applicable — UC MV uses explicit on: SQL conditions
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Full SQL expression. Con: More verbose and error-prone than entity-based automatic resolution.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (entities[] with type: primary | foreign | unique | natural)
Other Platform Notes: AtScale SML: relationship declarations with cardinality; LookML: join with relationship type

Description: First-class metric type defining a metric as numerator/denominator — both referencing other metrics or measures with optional filters and aliases.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ratio metrics are the most common KPI pattern (conversion rate, margin %, etc.); defining them declaratively prevents agents from incorrectly aggregating numerator and denominator separately.
Human Importance: High — ratio metrics surface as first-class metrics in BI tools alongside simple metrics
Agentic Importance: High — declarative numerator/denominator prevents agents from computing ratio metrics incorrectly; without this agents may average ratios instead of dividing aggregated totals
Snowflake SV Equivalent: metrics[].expr can express ratios as SQL division
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Ratio expressed as raw SQL; no structural declaration that this is a ratio metric; agents cannot introspect numerator/denominator semantically.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: MEASURE() composition makes the ratio structure explicit and self-documenting. Con: Less formally typed than dbt's ratio metric type.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (type: ratio with numerator / denominator — dbt MetricFlow)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].type: number referencing two other measures
Other Platform Notes: AtScale SML: calculated members; Power BI: DAX ratio measure; ThoughtSpot: safe_divide formula

Description: First-class metric type for rolling window (trailing N periods) and grain-to-date accumulation patterns; resets at grain boundaries.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Cumulative metrics (YTD revenue, 30-day rolling average) are essential business metrics; declaring them as a type ensures agents compute them with the correct window semantics rather than raw SUM.
Human Importance: High — YTD/MTD/rolling metrics are among the most frequently requested by business users
Agentic Importance: High — without a cumulative metric type agents must write raw window SQL; declarative type guarantees correct window semantics at every query
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Window SQL must be written manually; no structural type declaration. Pro: Full SQL flexibility.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Declarative cumulative config in UC MV. Pro: range: cumulative is semantically clear to agents.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (type: cumulative with window / grain_to_date / period_agg — dbt MetricFlow)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: type: running_total measure; ThoughtSpot: cumulative_sum formula

Description: First-class metric type defining a metric via arithmetic over other metrics with support for time offsets (period-over-period).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Derived metrics (revenue growth %, gross margin) and period-over-period calculations (MoM, YoY) are common executive KPIs; declaring them declaratively ensures agents compute them from governed atomic metrics.
Human Importance: High — period-over-period growth metrics are among the most requested executive-level metrics
Agentic Importance: High — offset_window and offset_to_grain enable period-comparison metrics that agents can query without raw LAG() SQL; declarative definition prevents incorrect period alignment
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No formal derived metric type or period-offset concept in SV YAML.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No period-offset concept in UC MV; period-over-period requires manual SQL or a separate time-shifted measure.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (type: derived with input_metrics[] / offset_window / offset_to_grain — dbt MetricFlow)
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not natively supported — requires date dimension manipulation
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: calculation groups for time intelligence (YTD, QTD, prior period) — more powerful for human-facing BI

Description: Tracks how often an entity that performs a base event subsequently performs a conversion event within a time window.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Conversion funnel metrics (checkout → purchase; signup → activation) are critical product and marketing KPIs; declarative conversion metrics prevent agents from computing funnels incorrectly.
Human Importance: High — conversion metrics are essential for product and marketing analysis; declarative definition makes them queryable without complex funnel SQL
Agentic Importance: High — conversion tracking requires session-level join logic; a declarative type ensures agents compute this correctly without writing event-sequence SQL
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Conversion metrics must be expressed as raw SQL subqueries or CTEs; no declarative type.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Conversion metrics must be computed via raw SQL; no declarative type in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (type: conversion with entity / calculation / base / conversion / window / constant_properties — dbt MetricFlow)
Other Platform Notes: No equivalent in ThoughtSpot; requires complex formula; no equivalent in Snowflake SV or Databricks MV

Description: Pre-defined reusable metric queries with materialization targets (table or view) written to the warehouse on a schedule.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables agents to query pre-aggregated metric tables directly for performance; creates governed datasets that downstream BI tools and agents can use without re-computing metrics from raw data.
Human Importance: High — exported saved queries create warehouse tables that humans can discover and query in any BI tool
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents can query materialized metric tables via SQL; reduces real-time compute cost but adds latency for fresh data
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not directly supported in SV YAML
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Semantic views are always query-time; no pre-materialization to a separate table from the semantic layer definition.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Experimental; limited control over aggregation strategy compared to dbt's export approach.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (saved_queries[].exports[].config.export_as: table | view — dbt MetricFlow)
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: aggregated_models; LookML: PDTs as saved query outputs

Description: Declares that a measure should not be summed across a specific time dimension; specifies window_choice (min/max) and optional window_groupings.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Prevents agents from incorrectly summing snapshot metrics (account balances, headcount, inventory levels) across time; this is one of the most common sources of silently wrong metric results.
Human Importance: Low — internal declaration; users see correct snapshot values without being aware of the constraint
Agentic Importance: High — arguably the most impactful correctness property for financial and operational metrics; without it agents sum snapshot values producing inflated totals
Snowflake SV Equivalent: NON ADDITIVE BY clause on metrics (Snowflake DDL) or non_additive_dimensions[] in YAML
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Full sort direction and null order control. Pro: Cortex Analyst natively generates correct LAST_VALUE SQL.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Declarative semiadditive config; cleaner than ThoughtSpot's is_additive flag.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (measure.non_additive_dimension.name / window_choice / window_groupings — dbt MetricFlow)
Other Platform Notes: AtScale SML: semi-additive as first-class measure subtype; Power BI: LASTDATE / FIRSTDATE DAX patterns

Description: Marks dimensions as valid_from and valid_to columns for Slowly Changing Dimension Type II support — MetricFlow correctly joins on the validity window.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables historically accurate reporting where dimension values change over time (e.g. customer region, product category, employee manager); without SCD support agents join on current state and produce wrong historical results.
Human Importance: Medium — SCD Type II is important for finance and HR reporting where historical accuracy matters
Agentic Importance: High — without SCD-aware joins agents produce point-in-time errors on historical reports; incorrect dimension snapshots cause audit and compliance failures
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV has no SCD-aware join support; historical analysis requires pre-built SCD views.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no SCD-aware join support.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (dimension.validity_params.is_start / is_end — dbt MetricFlow)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: handles SCD via Power Query M transformations; LookML: requires custom SQL for SCD joins

Description: Arbitrary key-value metadata attached to any semantic layer object (semantic_model, metric, dimension, measure, entity, saved_query) — compiled into manifest.json and surfaced to API consumers.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables teams to attach agent-specific hints, ownership, domain, sensitivity, and any custom metadata to semantic objects without changing the schema; the most flexible governance property across any platform.
Human Importance: Low — meta is developer-facing; not shown directly to business users
Agentic Importance: High — meta allows teams to attach agent-specific instructions (e.g. meta.agent_intent: 'use for revenue analysis') and sensitivity flags without schema changes; no equivalent in ThoughtSpot, Snowflake SV, or Databricks MV
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV has no arbitrary metadata passthrough. AI_SQL_GENERATION provides some equivalent at the view level only.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no metadata passthrough field. UC tags are closest but require separate DDL commands.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (meta: object on semantic_model / metric / dimension / measure / entity — dbt MetricFlow)
Cube.dev Equivalent: Native (meta: object on cube / dimension / measure / view — Cube.dev)
Other Platform Notes: No equivalent in ThoughtSpot or warehouse-native semantic views; unique to code-first platforms (dbt, Cube)

Description: Fills missing time periods with a specified value (e.g. 0) by joining the metric output to a dense time spine; ensures continuous time series without gaps.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Prevents agents from returning incomplete time series where periods with no activity are absent; a missing period is visually misleading in trend analysis and causes incorrect period-over-period calculations.
Human Importance: High — users expect continuous date axes in trend charts; gaps cause chart rendering issues
Agentic Importance: High — gap-filled time series are critical for cumulative metrics and period-over-period calculations; agents querying sparse metrics get incorrect results without time spine joins
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No time spine concept in Snowflake SV. Gaps in time series must be handled by the BI tool or a pre-built calendar table join.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No fill_nulls_with equivalent in UC MV. Continuous time series requires a calendar dimension join in the metric view.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (join_to_timespine + fill_nulls_with on simple / ratio / derived / cumulative metrics — dbt MetricFlow)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: time intelligence functions handle this; LookML: allow_fill on dimensions

Description: Shifts an input metric backward in time (e.g. 1 month) within a derived metric definition to enable period-over-period calculations.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Declarative period-over-period metrics (MoM, YoY, WoW) that agents can query without writing raw LAG() or self-join SQL; ensures correct date alignment at the query grain.
Human Importance: High — MoM and YoY metrics are among the most commonly requested metrics in any business context
Agentic Importance: High — offset_window prevents agents from writing incorrect period-comparison SQL; declarative definition guarantees correct temporal alignment
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Period-over-period requires raw SQL self-joins or CTEs in Snowflake SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No period-offset concept in UC MV; requires manual SQL or a separate time-shifted measure definition.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native (input_metrics[].offset_window / offset_to_grain on derived metrics — dbt MetricFlow)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: calculation groups with time intelligence; LookML: derived_column with date math

Description: Named reusable filter predicates stored in the data model (e.g. paid_orders: status = 'paid') — applied by name in queries without repeating the SQL.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: DRY filter definitions enable consistent audience segmentation across all metrics; agents and users reference segments by name rather than writing SQL conditions — reduces filter inconsistency.
Human Importance: High — named segments (e.g. 'Active Customers') surface as queryable filter options in BI and AI interfaces
Agentic Importance: High — named segments allow agents to apply consistent filter conditions by name rather than generating raw SQL conditions; reduces filter hallucination risk
Snowflake SV Equivalent: cube.segments[].sql (partial equivalent via WHERE clause in wrapper view)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Named reusable filters are not a Snowflake SV concept; filters must be embedded in metric expr or view wrappers.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported as a named concept
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has a single global filter: not named segmented filters.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter with Jinja conditions (partial equivalent per-metric)
Cube.dev Equivalent: Native (cube.segments[] — name + sql + description + public — Cube.dev)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: named filters as a dimension type; ThoughtSpot: model-level filters (not named/reusable)

Description: Declarative rollup materialization definitions embedded in the data model — Cube manages creation, refresh, and query routing automatically.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Transparent query acceleration for agents and humans; frequently queried metric rollups are pre-computed in the warehouse and automatically selected at query time without changing queries.
Human Importance: High — dramatically improves response time for common aggregations; users see sub-second results on large datasets
Agentic Importance: Low — agents benefit from faster responses but do not interact with pre_aggregation definitions directly
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not directly in SV YAML (use Snowflake dynamic tables or materialized views separately)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV has no built-in pre-aggregation management; acceleration is external to the semantic layer.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC materialization is experimental and less feature-rich than Cube's pre-aggregation system.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[].exports[] for materialization; no in-model pre-aggregation scheduling
Cube.dev Equivalent: Native (cube.pre_aggregations[] — type / measures / dimensions / time_dimension / granularity / partition_granularity / refresh_key — Cube.dev)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: PDTs (Persistent Derived Tables) — closest equivalent; ThoughtSpot: aggregated_models

Description: Declarative rolling window aggregation on a measure (trailing N periods / leading N periods / unbounded) with offset control.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables 7-day rolling average, trailing 30-day revenue, and similar window aggregations as first-class measures; agents can query rolling metrics by name without knowing the underlying OVER() SQL.
Human Importance: High — rolling window metrics (7-day rolling sales, trailing 3-month average) are essential for trend analysis
