Agentic Importance: High — declarative rolling windows allow agents to query temporal patterns correctly without generating window SQL; reduces agent query complexity significantly
Snowflake SV Equivalent: NON ADDITIVE BY / window SQL in metrics[].expr (manual)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No declarative rolling window; must write OVER() SQL manually in metrics[].expr.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native trailing window config in UC MV v1.1. Pro: Cleaner than raw SQL for agents to interpret.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric type: cumulative with window parameter (closest equivalent)
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: moving_average / moving_sum formulas; LookML: type: rolling_window measure

Description: Row-level and member-level security policies embedded in the data model; user attributes drive dynamic filter conditions and field visibility.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Governance at the semantic layer; agents querying through Cube automatically receive filtered views appropriate to their user context without requiring separate model versions.
Human Importance: Medium — users see only data and fields permitted by their role; security is transparent
Agentic Importance: High — agents operating under user identities receive correctly scoped data automatically; no separate security layer required between the agent and the semantic model
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not supported in SV YAML (Snowflake role grants and row access policies handle this)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Security is external to the semantic view definition; must be managed separately.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported in UC MV YAML (UC privilege system and row filters handle this)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Security is at the UC catalog level not the metric view level.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt Semantic Layer access controlled by API credentials and job permissions
Cube.dev Equivalent: Native (cube.access_policy[].group / groups / member_level / row_level / filters — Cube.dev)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: access_filter + required_access_grants; Power BI: RLS + OLS; ThoughtSpot: RLS via is_bypass_rls flag

Description: Named ordered drill-down paths within a cube (e.g. Year → Quarter → Month → Day; Country → Region → City).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables structured exploratory drill-down; agents and BI tools can traverse defined hierarchies without knowing which columns to drill through at each level.
Human Importance: High — drill-down hierarchies are fundamental to human data exploration; essential for geographic and temporal navigation
Agentic Importance: Low — agents typically receive explicit dimension requests not hierarchical drill paths; hierarchy support in agent interfaces is limited
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No hierarchy concept in Snowflake SV; drill paths must be defined in the BI tool.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No hierarchy concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt dimensions define the grain but not explicit drill hierarchies
Other Platform Notes: LookML: dimension_group (time hierarchies); AtScale SML: explicit hierarchy objects with level definitions; Power BI: hierarchies on tables; ThoughtSpot: no formal hierarchy definition

Description: Named consumer-facing compositions of cube measures and dimensions; expose curated subsets of the cube graph with aliasing, inclusion/exclusion, and folder organization.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Audience-specific views of the semantic model allow agents to select the right scope without knowing the full cube structure; reduces the surface area an agent needs to reason over.
Human Importance: High — views provide curated subject-area interfaces that simplify human exploration; a 'Finance View' hides fields irrelevant to finance users
Agentic Importance: High — views reduce the schema surface area agents must reason over; a narrower more relevant view produces more accurate agent queries than querying the full cube directly
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV has no view/facade/perspective concept; all dimensions/metrics are always exposed.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no perspective or facade concept; all dimensions/measures are always exposed.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt semantic models can be scoped by metric definition
Cube.dev Equivalent: Native (cube.views[] with cubes[].join_path / includes / excludes / folders — Cube.dev)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: explores as audience-specific facades; AtScale SML: perspectives; Power BI: perspectives

Description: Inherits all members (dimensions, measures, joins) from another cube or view — enables DRY reuse without duplication.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Reduces maintenance burden in large models; agents benefit from consistent definitions inherited from a base cube rather than diverging duplicated definitions.
Human Importance: Low — inheritance is a modeling pattern; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Low — inherited definitions behave identically to inline definitions from an agent's perspective
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No inheritance concept in Snowflake SV; logic must be duplicated across multiple views.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No inheritance concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: LookML: extends and refinements on views and explores — more powerful inheritance pattern

Description: Specifies which configured database connection a cube uses — enables a single semantic model to span multiple data warehouses.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Multi-warehouse models allow agents to query a unified semantic interface while data physically resides in different systems (Snowflake for historical, Databricks for streaming, Postgres for operational).
Human Importance: Low — users see a unified view; data_source routing is transparent
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents query a consistent semantic model regardless of where data resides; simplifies agent architecture for multi-warehouse environments
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV can only reference Snowflake objects; no cross-warehouse federation.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV can only reference Unity Catalog objects; no cross-warehouse federation.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt models reference one data platform per project
Other Platform Notes: No equivalent in warehouse-native semantic layers; unique to headless semantic layer platforms (Cube, AtScale)

Description: Defines a single timestamp column and automatically generates multiple temporal dimensions (raw, date, week, month, quarter, year, day_of_week, etc.) from it.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Eliminates the need to manually define separate date/week/month dimensions from the same timestamp; agents can query any grain from a single timestamp column declaration.
Human Importance: High — users see a clean date picker with all granularities available without separate columns
Agentic Importance: High — automatic grain fan-out ensures agents always have the correct granularity-specific column available; prevents agents from writing date_trunc SQL manually
Snowflake SV Equivalent: time_dimensions[] with a single expr per grain (manual)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Each grain must be defined as a separate time_dimension entry or the BI tool must handle granularity.
Databricks UC Equivalent: dimensions[] with DATE_TRUNC expressions (manual per grain)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Each granularity requires a separate dimension with explicit DATE_TRUNC expr.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dimension.type_params.time_granularity sets minimum grain; MetricFlow auto-generates grain variants at query time
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.dimensions[].granularities[] — custom granularity definitions on time dimensions
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: date hierarchy auto-generation; ThoughtSpot: default_date_bucket for grain hints

Description: Custom HTML template applied to dimension and measure values — enables conditional colors, images, external links, and badges in cells.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables rich data presentation that communicates context directly in the table cell (e.g. red/green traffic lights on KPIs); reduces the need for users to manually interpret raw values.
Human Importance: High — conditional cell formatting is one of the most impactful UX features for human data consumption
Agentic Importance: Low — agents produce text or structured data responses; HTML rendering is irrelevant to agent output
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No HTML rendering concept in Snowflake SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No HTML rendering concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: conditional formatting rules; ThoughtSpot: no HTML rendering in semantic layer

Description: Declares which dimensions to expose when a user clicks through (drills) on a measure value.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Defines structured exploration paths from a high-level metric to underlying detail; agents with drill-down capability can follow these paths to explain metric anomalies.
Human Importance: High — drill-through is one of the most used BI exploration features; defines the click-through experience
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents with investigative capability can use drill_fields to navigate from an anomalous metric to its underlying rows
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Drill paths must be defined in the BI tool.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No drill path concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].drill_members[] — dimensions to expose on drill-down
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: drill-through configured in the report layer; ThoughtSpot: drill anywhere is automatic

Description: Webhooks and API calls triggered from a data cell in the Looker UI — enables write-back and workflow automation from a data point.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Connects data insights to actions (e.g. create a Salesforce task from a metric anomaly); blurs the line between analytics and operational systems.
Human Importance: High — action buttons in tables enable users to trigger workflows directly from data without leaving the BI tool
Agentic Importance: Low — few agents currently support write-back actions from semantic layer queries; primarily a human-facing feature
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No action concept in Snowflake SV; actions must be implemented in the application layer.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No action concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: no declarative data actions in semantic layer; Atlan: MCP tools support write operations to update metadata

Description: Row-level security dynamically applied based on user attributes (e.g. user.region limits rows to the user's assigned region) — embedded in the explore definition.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Declarative user-context-aware data filtering embedded in the semantic model; agents operating under user identities automatically see correctly filtered data without separate security logic.
Human Importance: Low — security is transparent to users; they see only their permitted rows
Agentic Importance: High — user-attribute-driven filters allow agents to operate securely under end-user identities; critical for multi-tenant and personalized agent deployments
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Row access policies are applied at the Snowflake account/table level not the semantic view definition.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported in UC MV YAML
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Row filters are applied via Unity Catalog policies not inside the metric view definition.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — security via dbt Cloud API tokens
Other Platform Notes: LookML: access_filter is the most expressive equivalent; ThoughtSpot: RLS policies in platform settings

Description: A hardcoded WHERE clause applied to every query on an explore — invisible to users and cannot be overridden.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures data governance restrictions (e.g. is_deleted = false) are always applied regardless of how the explore is queried; agents cannot bypass this filter even if they try.
Human Importance: Low — invisible to users by design; ensures clean data without user awareness
Agentic Importance: High — invisible mandatory filters are the strongest governance guardrail for agent queries; agents cannot accidentally query deleted or restricted records
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Must be implemented as a Snowflake row access policy or view wrapper; no invisible filter at the semantic layer level.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: UC's top-level filter is effectively a mandatory always-where; agents cannot bypass it.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter on all metrics (creates per-metric mandatory filter)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.pre_aggregations with segments[] (close but not invisible)
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: model-level filters with is_mandatory: true — closest equivalent

Description: Marks a semantic object as Verified / Draft / Deprecated by a designated data steward — surfaced as a trust badge in catalogs and BI tools.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Tells agents and users whether a metric or model is production-ready and authorised for business reporting; prevents agents from citing unverified or deprecated metrics in executive reports.
Human Importance: High — users need to know whether a metric is officially verified before using it in a presentation or report
Agentic Importance: High — agents querying unverified metrics may produce results that are later disputed; trust status is critical for agentic reliability in high-stakes contexts
Snowflake SV Equivalent: AI_VERIFIED_QUERIES with verified_at / verified_by (query-level verification only)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Verified Q&A pairs provide examples. Con: No object-level certification on the semantic view itself.
Databricks UC Equivalent: UC object-level certification (set via Databricks catalog UI — not a YAML property)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Certification visible in lineage panels and dashboards. Con: Not embedded in the metric view YAML definition; managed separately.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported natively
Other Platform Notes: Atlan: certificate status (VERIFIED / DRAFT / DEPRECATED) as a first-class asset property; OpenMetadata: certification status; Power BI: endorsement (Certified / Promoted)

Description: Links a table/column/metric to a canonical business glossary term (e.g. 'Revenue' → glossary term with official definition, synonyms, and steward).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Provides agents with authoritative business definitions linked at the column level; an agent that knows 'Revenue' is linked to the official glossary term with a specific calculation rule produces more accurate and defensible answers.
Human Importance: High — glossary links help data literacy; users can click through to the official definition of a metric
Agentic Importance: High — glossary term definitions are the richest source of business context for agents; more authoritative than ad-hoc ai_context descriptions
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No glossary link concept in Snowflake SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No glossary link concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported — dbt descriptions are the closest analog
Other Platform Notes: Atlan: meanings[] (linked glossary terms); OpenMetadata: glossaryTerms[]; ThoughtSpot: no business glossary integration in the semantic layer

Description: Broadcast messages attached to a semantic object (e.g. 'Deprecating next quarter' / 'Calculation changed on Jan 1') — visible to all consumers.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Alerts agents and users to upcoming changes, known issues, or important context without requiring users to check documentation; critical for managing semantic layer changes gracefully.
Human Importance: High — announcements surface as banners in BI tools and catalog UIs; ensures users see important change notifications
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents consuming catalog APIs can read announcement metadata; useful for routing queries away from deprecated models
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No announcement concept in Snowflake SV or any warehouse-native semantic layer.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No announcement concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported
Other Platform Notes: Atlan: announcements with type (information / warning / issue) + title + message — the most comprehensive implementation

Property: Governance: README / long-form documentation per asset
Description: Markdown documentation attached to a semantic object at the asset level — explains the business context, calculation methodology, known limitations, and usage guidance.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Provides agents with rich contextual knowledge that cannot fit in a description field; a well-written README can explain fiscal year definitions, exclusion rules, data quality caveats, and recommended usage patterns.
Human Importance: High — README documents appear in catalog UIs and data portals; critical for human data literacy and self-service adoption
Agentic Importance: High — LLMs consuming README content can generate significantly more accurate answers on complex or nuanced metrics; the richest source of unstructured semantic context available
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: SV description field is limited to a single string; no markdown documentation support.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV comment is limited to a single string; no markdown documentation support.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported — dbt descriptions support Markdown but are typically concise
Other Platform Notes: Atlan: README per asset (full Markdown) — the most comprehensive implementation; OpenMetadata: description supports Markdown; ThoughtSpot: no README equivalent

Property: Governance: PII / sensitivity tags with lineage propagation
Description: Data classification tags (PII / Confidential / Restricted) that propagate automatically through data lineage to all downstream assets.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Prevents agents from inadvertently exposing sensitive data in query results; PII-tagged columns can trigger masking policies or agent refusal to include them in responses.
Human Importance: Low — users are generally unaware of PII tags; they experience the effect as masked or absent data
Agentic Importance: High — agents must know which columns contain PII to comply with data protection requirements; lineage propagation ensures newly derived metrics inherit PII classification automatically
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake column masking policies provide PII protection but are managed separately from the semantic view; no auto-propagation through the semantic layer.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported in UC MV YAML
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC column masks and row filters protect PII but are managed at the catalog level not the metric view definition.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported
Other Platform Notes: Atlan: Atlan Tags with propagation through lineage; OpenMetadata: PII classification AI agent; ThoughtSpot: no PII classification in semantic layer

Property: Governance: data quality scores and SLAs
Description: Quality metrics (completeness, freshness, accuracy scores) and SLA definitions attached to semantic assets — surfaced to consumers as quality badges.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Tells agents whether the underlying data is trustworthy before generating a response; a metric with a failing freshness SLA should not be cited in a time-sensitive report.
Human Importance: High — data quality scores help users decide whether to trust a metric for a given use case
Agentic Importance: High — agents need quality signals to decide whether to proceed with a query or warn the user about data reliability; a stale or incomplete metric produces misleading agent responses
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No quality scoring concept in Snowflake SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No quality scoring concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported — dbt tests validate data quality but scores are not embedded in the semantic layer
Other Platform Notes: Atlan: DQ rules + scores + SLA definitions; OpenMetadata: data quality test suites linked at column level; ThoughtSpot: no data quality properties in semantic layer

Description: Usage signals (view count, query count, starred by, follower count) attached to semantic assets — surface the most-used metrics and tables.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Guides agents and users toward the metrics that the organization actually relies on; low-popularity metrics may be experimental or deprecated even if not formally marked as such.
Human Importance: High — popularity signals help users discover the most relevant and trusted metrics in large catalogs
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents can use popularity signals to rank multiple similar metrics and prefer the most-used one
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No usage tracking in Snowflake SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No usage tracking in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported
Other Platform Notes: Atlan: popularityScore / viewScore / starredBy; OpenMetadata: followers / votes; ThoughtSpot: usage analytics available via REST API but not embedded in semantic layer

Description: Dynamic time intelligence modifiers (YTD, QTD, prior period, rolling 12M, currency conversion) applied to all measures at once via a single definition — no measure duplication needed.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Eliminates measure explosion for time intelligence; agents can apply any time intelligence pattern to any measure by referencing the calculation group item name.
Human Importance: High — one of the most powerful BI modeling features for human self-service; enables time comparisons without creating dozens of separate measures
Agentic Importance: High — agents can apply named time intelligence patterns (e.g. 'YTD Revenue', 'vs Prior Month') to any measure without the model defining each combination explicitly
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV requires a separate metric for each time intelligence variant; no dynamic application pattern.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV requires separate window measures for each time variant; no dynamic application.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric type: derived with offset_window provides period-over-period (partial equivalent)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: calculation groups (strongest implementation); LookML: liquid templating for dynamic SQL; dbt MetricFlow: derived metrics with offset_window

Description: Named audience-specific subsets of the semantic model — 'Finance Perspective' exposes only finance-relevant tables, columns, and measures.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Allows agents to select the right scope for a query by audience context; a narrower perspective produces more accurate agent queries than reasoning over the full model.
Human Importance: High — perspectives simplify the field picker for different business teams; a focused interface improves human exploration
Agentic Importance: High — perspectives reduce the schema surface area an agent must reason over; a scoped model produces more accurate NL queries than a full enterprise model
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All SV dimensions/metrics are always exposed; no perspective/facade concept.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All UC MV dimensions/measures are always exposed.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — separate semantic models per audience provide a similar effect
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.views[] with cubes[].includes / excludes — closest Cube equivalent
Other Platform Notes: LookML: explores as audience-specific facades; AtScale SML: perspectives; Cube: views[]

Description: Locale-specific display names and descriptions for all model objects — enables the same semantic model to serve users in multiple languages.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: A single governed semantic model that automatically presents in the user's language; agents serving multilingual users can produce responses in the appropriate locale without maintaining separate models.
Human Importance: High — essential for multinational deployments where business users work in different languages
Agentic Importance: Low — most agents currently operate in a single language; i18n is rarely a priority for agentic interfaces
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No locale-specific display names in Snowflake SV; separate views per locale would be required.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No i18n support in UC MV YAML.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: translations for table/column/measure display names per locale; ThoughtSpot: locale aliases in column_alias_udf.tml (separate file); LookML: no native i18n

Property: Power BI: Q&A synonyms and linguistic schema
Description: Curated synonym lists and verb mappings specifically for the Power BI Q&A and Copilot natural-language engine — including phrasings (e.g. 'show me', 'list') and implied orderings.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: The most comprehensive NLP vocabulary specification of any platform; enables agents to resolve a wider range of natural-language phrasings to the correct semantic objects.
Human Importance: High — Q&A synonyms directly improve how accurately users' natural-language questions resolve to the correct data
Agentic Importance: High — linguistic schema is the richest form of NLP grounding; agents querying Power BI models via Copilot benefit from the full vocabulary definition
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV synonyms are simpler string arrays with no verb mappings or linguistic structure.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV synonyms are simple string arrays; no linguistic schema.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt MetricFlow handles NL via descriptions and labels
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: Q&A linguistic schema (strongest NLP specification); ThoughtSpot: synonyms[] (simpler but effective)

Description: Semantic type hints on columns: Country, City, PostalCode, Longitude, Latitude, URL, ImageURL, BarcodeUrl — used for map visuals and Copilot context.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Tells agents what kind of real-world entity a column represents; enables Copilot to generate map visualizations and URL rendering automatically from the semantic model.
Human Importance: High — geo dataCategory enables automatic map visualization rendering; URL/ImageURL enables rich media in tables
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents can use dataCategory to infer column semantics (e.g. use a City column as a geographic dimension in a map query)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No semantic type hints in Snowflake SV beyond the standard data_type (TEXT / NUMBER / DATE).
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No semantic type hints in UC MV beyond standard SQL types.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: dataCategory; ThoughtSpot: geo_config provides a similar but narrower geographic role classification

Description: Alternative names for a dimension or measure recognized by Looker's Conversational Analytics (Gemini) and Q&A engine — equivalent to ThoughtSpot synonyms but surfaced via the Semantic API.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Expands the NL vocabulary agents can use to resolve field references; particularly valuable for acronyms and business aliases that differ from technical column names.
Human Importance: High — synonyms improve Q&A and search accuracy for human users typing natural terms
Agentic Importance: High — consumed by Looker's AI and by external agents via the Semantic API; directly improves NL→field resolution accuracy
Snowflake SV Equivalent: synonyms[] on each dimension / time_dimension / metric
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Same pattern — array of alternative strings directly consumed by Cortex Analyst. Con: No synonym weighting.
Databricks UC Equivalent: synonyms[] on dimensions and measures (v1.1 — up to 10)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Same pattern consumed by Genie. Con: 10-synonym limit per field.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dimension.label + description carry synonym-like signals (no explicit synonyms array in MetricFlow)
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not natively supported — cube.title is the primary alias
Other Platform Notes: LookML: synonyms[] on any field or explore (requires new LookML runtime); Power BI: Q&A synonyms in linguistic schema; ThoughtSpot: synonyms[]

Description: A filter-only input field (not in SELECT) with typed values (string / number / unquoted / date_time / yesno); its value is injected into SQL expressions via Liquid {% parameter %} templating; supports allowed_value drop-down options and suggest_explore for dynamic value lookup.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables governed runtime parameterization of SQL expressions — dimension labels and SQL change based on user selection without separate model versions per scenario.
Human Importance: High — exposed as UI controls (dropdowns, date pickers) that users interact with to reshape queries
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents can inject parameter values at query time for dynamic SQL; requires knowing allowed_value options
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not directly supported (SV YAML has no runtime parameter concept)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Hard blocker — parameters require manual model variants in Snowflake SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Same hard blocker — no runtime parameter concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — MetricFlow uses metrics[].filter with Jinja conditions
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not applicable — Cube achieves this via separate cubes or user_attribute-driven access_policy
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: parameters[] (closest equivalent but also unsupported in Snowflake/Databricks targets)

Description: Project-wide reusable display format strings (Excel-style) defined once at the model level and referenced by name on any field via value_format_name; includes strict_value_format to prevent user number_format overrides.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures consistent number presentation across the entire semantic model without duplicating format strings on every field; a single model-wide change updates formatting everywhere.
Human Importance: High — consistent currency, percentage, and number formatting across all visualizations improves report readability
Agentic Importance: Low — display formatting is a BI concern; agents generate text or structured data responses where format strings are irrelevant
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No project-wide format registry in Snowflake SV; format strings in metrics[] descriptions or BI layer.
Databricks UC Equivalent: format: at the measure level (v1.1) provides field-level formatting
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No shared named format registry; each measure defines its own format object independently.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not supported
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: named format strings applied to measures via DAX FORMAT(); ThoughtSpot: format_pattern on each column (no shared registry)

Description: Named reusable query blocks defined inside an explore — specify dimensions, measures, filters, sorts, and limit; appear as clickable Quick Start suggestions in the Looker Explore UI.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Pre-defined sample queries lower the barrier to entry for human exploration; for agents they serve as validated NL→query pair examples showing what the explore can answer.
Human Importance: High — Quick Start suggestions directly guide human users toward the most common and valuable analyses
Agentic Importance: Medium — validated sample queries serve as few-shot examples that help agents understand what questions the explore is designed to answer
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: More AI-specific — includes verified_at timestamp, verified_by contact, and onboarding_question flag. Con: Not embedded in the SV definition itself (DDL clause / separate YAML section).
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No sample query concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[] — pre-defined metric queries (most similar dbt equivalent)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: Verified Answers in LSDL (trigger phrase + visual binding — most structured); ThoughtSpot: verified queries not yet available

Description: Named caching policies (sql_trigger / interval_trigger / cron + max_cache_age) that control when PDTs and explore caches are invalidated; referenced by explores and derived tables via persist_with.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Decouples cache lifecycle from query time — explores rebuild only when the underlying ETL completes, not on a fixed schedule; prevents stale cache serving while minimising rebuild cost.
Human Importance: High — cache freshness directly affects whether users see current data; aligns BI refresh with ETL completion
Agentic Importance: Low — caching is infrastructure-level; agents see the effect (fresh or stale data) but do not interact with datagroup definitions
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV is always query-time; no internal cache lifecycle concept. Acceleration via materialized views or dynamic tables managed separately.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV materialization schedule serves a similar purpose but is embedded in the YAML definition rather than a named shared policy.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt models use incremental strategy and scheduling
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.pre_aggregations[].refresh_key — equivalent cache invalidation logic per pre-aggregation
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: no equivalent semantic-layer-level cache policy

Description: Pre-defined rollup query blocks inside an explore that Looker can route to automatically when a query matches the aggregate's dimensions and measures — without the user knowing.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables Looker to route compatible queries to pre-aggregated tables for sub-second responses while serving the same semantic interface; agents and humans query identically but at different speeds.
Human Importance: High — transparent query acceleration; users get fast results without changing their queries
Agentic Importance: Low — agents benefit from faster responses but do not interact with aggregate_table definitions directly
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV has no semantic-layer-level aggregate routing. Acceleration via materialized views is external to the semantic view definition.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Native materialization config in UC MV — similar intent. Con: Less feature-rich than LookML aggregate awareness (no partial match routing, no fanout handling).
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[].exports[] materializes metric output; MetricFlow routes queries to materialized exports
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.pre_aggregations[] — full aggregate awareness with automatic query routing
Other Platform Notes: AtScale SML: model.aggregates[] — explicit user-defined aggregates with automatic routing; ThoughtSpot: aggregated_models

Description: View definition backed by a SQL query or explore_source (not a physical table); supports persistence configurations including datagroup_trigger, interval_trigger, persist_for, incremental rebuild (increment_key + increment_offset), partition_keys, cluster_keys, and publish_as_db_view.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables complex pre-transformations and materializations within the semantic layer; agents query derived tables exactly like physical tables without knowing the underlying SQL.
Human Importance: High — derived tables allow modeling logic that cannot be expressed in simple column references; critical for complex business metrics
Agentic Importance: Low — agents interact with derived tables as opaque physical-like sources; the persistence config is infrastructure not agent-facing
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All SV tables must be physical objects in Snowflake; views can be referenced but the materialization must be managed outside the SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV source can be a SQL SELECT (inline derived) but full PDT-style materialization requires a separate notebook or pipeline.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dbt models (ref() tables) serve the same role — transformations external to the semantic layer
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.pre_aggregations[] with type: original_sql — materializes the cube's base SQL
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: sql_view handles simple derived queries but not the full PDT feature set

Description: Reusable named lists of fields defined at the view level; referenced via the * suffix (e.g. orders.core_fields*) in explore.fields, join.fields, measure.drill_fields, and view.drill_fields; supports set algebra with ALL_FIELDS* and - exclusion.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables consistent field whitelisting and drill path definitions across the semantic model; changing a set updates every reference point simultaneously — critical for large models with many explores.
Human Importance: High — sets control what fields are visible in each explore and drill path; wrong sets cause missing or excessive fields for users
Agentic Importance: Low — agents query available fields not field sets directly; sets affect the visible surface but agents adapt to whatever is exposed
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No field set concept in Snowflake SV — all dimensions/metrics are individually defined.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No field set concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt exposes all measures/dimensions in a semantic model
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.views[].cubes[].includes/excludes — closest equivalent (include/exclude specific members per view)
Other Platform Notes: AtScale SML: perspectives[] hide mechanism provides similar scoping; LookML: sets are the most flexible field-grouping mechanism

Description: Modifies an existing LookML object in-place using + prefix syntax without creating a new named object — additive for joins/access_filters/aggregate_tables; overriding for labels/descriptions; supports final: yes guard to prevent further modification.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables model layering: a base project defines the core semantic model; refinements add client-specific or environment-specific overlays without forking the base. Agents consuming the compiled model see the merged result.
Human Importance: Low — refinements are a developer concern; users and agents see the compiled model
Agentic Importance: Low — agents interact with the merged compiled model; refinements are invisible at runtime
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No model overlay/refinement concept in Snowflake SV; changes require updating the view definition directly.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No overlay pattern in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt achieves this via model overrides and environment-specific configurations
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.extends property on views — inherits all members from another cube or view
Other Platform Notes: LookML refinements are unique; closest analog is AtScale SML package imports for cross-project extension

Description: Declares that other specified fields must be selected whenever this field is selected — prevents incomplete or misleading results when a field only makes sense in context.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures agents and users cannot query a metric in isolation when its correct interpretation requires a companion field (e.g. a ratio measure that requires a denominator dimension to be meaningful).
Human Importance: Medium — prevents accidental misuse of context-dependent fields
Agentic Importance: High — without required_fields an agent could query a ratio metric without the required grouping dimension and receive a single misleading number
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No field dependency declaration in Snowflake SV; agents can query any metric independently.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No field dependency declaration in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: no equivalent field dependency declaration

Description: Named unit tests that run a LookML query and assert expressions against the results — validate that model logic produces expected values before deployment.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Provides automated semantic correctness verification; prevents silent metric regressions from reaching users and agents by catching calculation errors at model deploy time.
Human Importance: Low — tests run at deploy time; users never see them directly
Agentic Importance: High — validated model logic gives agents a trustworthy foundation; undetected regressions cause silent wrong answers at every query
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No declarative unit test framework in Snowflake SV; correctness must be validated via ad-hoc Cortex Analyst queries.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No unit test framework in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: dbt tests + data quality assertions on columns (closest equivalent — validates source data not metric logic)
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: no semantic layer unit test framework; correctness validated via ad-hoc Spotter queries

Description: Physical source definition (table or SQL query) with explicit column declarations including data_type, calculated columns via sql expression, dialect-specific SQL variants, and incremental build configuration (column + grace_period).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Separates physical source definition from logical semantic objects; allows a single physical table to be referenced multiple times with different column selections and dialect-specific SQL without duplicating business logic.
Human Importance: Low — internal source binding; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Low — agents interact with the semantic model not the physical source definition; dataset objects are plumbing
Snowflake SV Equivalent: base_table: {database, schema, table} — FQN reference without column declaration
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV does not declare columns at the table level; column definitions live in dimensions/metrics. Pro: Less verbose for simple cases.
Databricks UC Equivalent: source: catalog.schema.table — FQN reference without column declaration
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Same — UC MV references tables without explicit column schemas. Pro: Simpler for non-complex cases.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: model: ref('dbt_model') — references a compiled dbt model with full column schema
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.sql_table or cube.sql — references physical source without explicit column declarations
Other Platform Notes: LookML: view with sql_table_name or derived_table; Power BI: table with column definitions

Description: Defines a named database+schema connection that datasets reference; separates infrastructure credentials from logical definitions; multiple schemas require separate connection objects.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables clean separation of physical infrastructure from semantic modeling; teams can switch the underlying database connection without changing semantic object definitions.
Human Importance: Low — internal infrastructure; transparent to users
Agentic Importance: Low — agents interact with the model not connection objects
Snowflake SV Equivalent: base_table in each table entry serves as the connection reference
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Each SV table independently specifies database/schema — no shared connection object.
Databricks UC Equivalent: source: in each metric view — no shared connection object
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Same — UC MV does not have a shared connection registry in the YAML.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: profiles.yml / dbt_project.yml handle connection configuration outside the semantic model
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.data_source per cube — names a configured connection
Other Platform Notes: LookML: connection at model level; ThoughtSpot: connection object at table level

Description: Defines the physical columns backing each hierarchy level — key_columns[], name_column, sort_column, is_unique_key, contains_unique_names; also controls aggregate inclusion via exclude_from_dim_agg and exclude_from_fact_agg; supports custom_empty_member for missing FK handling.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Fine-grained control over how dimension members are identified, displayed, and sorted; is_unique_key and contains_unique_names enable MDX-correct member resolution across many-to-many patterns.
Human Importance: Low — key column declarations are developer-facing infrastructure
Agentic Importance: Medium — correct key declarations determine whether agents get unique unambiguous member names; custom_empty_member prevents null/missing FK members from breaking hierarchical queries
Snowflake SV Equivalent: dimensions[] in SV reference physical columns via expr
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No key/name/sort column separation in SV; the single expr serves all roles. Pro: Simpler for most cases.
Databricks UC Equivalent: dimensions[].expr — single expression serves as both key and display value
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No separation of key and display columns; name collisions require wrapper views.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: entity.expr supports composite keys via SQL concat; dimension.name_column concept is not present
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not supported — Cube references columns directly in sql expressions
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: column isKey property + sortByColumn — closest equivalent for key/display separation

Description: Declares the default MDX member that is applied when a dimension is in the query scope but not explicitly filtered — expressed as an MDX expression; apply_only_when_in_query controls scope.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Ensures consistent default aggregation scope across MDX and tabular queries; without a default_member agents may receive unexpected aggregate totals when dimensions are in-scope without explicit filter selection.
Human Importance: High — default member determines what data users see when they open a report without selecting a filter value
Agentic Importance: Medium — default member affects the scope of agent queries when dimensions are present but unfiltered; incorrect defaults produce misleading total values
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No default member concept in Snowflake SV; the full table is always queried unless a filter is specified.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No default member concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — MetricFlow always queries all data unless metrics[].filter is applied
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: calculated member as a default; AtScale: strongest default_member implementation with MDX expression and apply_only_when_in_query control

Description: Declares a role-playing dimension where a single physical dimension (e.g. a date dimension) is reused multiple times under different labels (Order Date / Ship Date / Delivery Date) via a name template (e.g. '{0} Date' / 'Order {0}').
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Single source of truth for shared dimension definitions; agents see multiple distinct dimension contexts from one physical table without duplication or risk of divergent definitions.
Human Importance: High — role-played dimensions provide distinct date context labels (Order Date vs Ship Date) that users rely on to build correct reports
Agentic Importance: High — role-play generates distinct prefixed dimension hierarchies that agents can correctly distinguish without join ambiguity; prevents dimension confusion in multi-role-date models
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not directly supported (requires separate wrapper views)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV requires a physically distinct wrapper view for each role — more verbose and drift-prone.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not directly supported (multiple joins[] entries with distinct names)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV requires distinct join alias names per role; same physical table referenced multiple times via separate joins.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: entity.role attribute on entities — disambiguates multiple FK relationships to the same entity type
Other Platform Notes: LookML: from: on a join aliases the same view under different join names; AtScale role_play is more declarative

Description: Defines calculation groups (dynamic metric modifiers) embedded within a dimension — each group has calculated_members with built-in templates (Current / Previous / YTD / MTD / QTD / Moving Average 3/5/30 Period / Pct of Total / Pct of Parent / Last Year / YoY%) or custom MDX expressions; precedence controls evaluation order when multiple calculation groups interact.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Built-in time intelligence and statistical templates provide governed period-over-period and moving average logic that agents can apply to any metric by selecting the calculation group member — no metric-specific duplication.
Human Importance: High — calculation groups (e.g. YTD / vs Prior Year) are the most requested pattern in executive reporting; built-in templates make them available on every metric instantly
Agentic Importance: High — agents can request 'revenue Year to Date' without a specific YTD measure being defined; the calculation group applies the logic dynamically to any compatible measure
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV has no calculation group concept; each time intelligence variant requires a separate metric definition.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no calculation group concept; window[] on measures is the closest but applies to single measures not groups.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric type: derived with offset_window provides period-over-period (closest; less reusable)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: calculation groups (very similar concept — same origin from Microsoft SSAS); LookML: liquid templating for dynamic SQL

Description: Declares that a metric cannot be aggregated across a specific dimension (e.g. account_balance cannot be summed across snapshot_date); specifies position (first / last / first_child / last_child) and the relevant dimensional relationships.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Prevents the most common class of silent metric error in financial and operational data — summing snapshot balances across time periods; first-class semi-additive declaration ensures correct aggregation at every query.
Human Importance: Low — internal aggregation rule; users see correct snapshot values without being aware of the constraint
Agentic Importance: High — semi-additive correctness is critical for finance, HR, and inventory metrics; without it agents sum snapshot values producing inflated results on every time-series query
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Cortex Analyst natively generates correct LAST_VALUE / FIRST_VALUE SQL. Con: Sort direction and null order must be specified explicitly.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Declarative first/last control. Con: Experimental status; limited to current range semantics.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measure.non_additive_dimension.window_choice: min | max
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: LASTDATE / LASTNONBLANK DAX patterns; AtScale SML: most explicit semi-additive configuration with position + relationship linking

Description: Aggregation function for a metric — extends beyond standard SUM/AVG/COUNT to include: estimated count distinct (HyperLogLog), sum distinct (fanout-safe), percentile (with compression / named_quantiles / custom_quantiles), stddev_pop, stddev_samp, var_pop, var_samp, count non-null.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Statistical aggregation types beyond SUM/COUNT give analysts and agents access to distribution metrics (percentile, stddev) and fanout-safe aggregations from a governed semantic definition.
Human Importance: High — percentile and standard deviation metrics are increasingly requested for operational analytics; governed definitions prevent inconsistent calculations
Agentic Importance: High — stddev and percentile are high-value statistical metrics; a declarative calculation_method ensures agents compute them identically to the business definition regardless of which query tool is used
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Full SQL flexibility — any aggregation expressible in SQL. Con: Percentile and stddev must be written as raw SQL expressions; no named method with parameters.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Same SQL flexibility. Con: Same limitation — no named method; percentile requires PERCENTILE_CONT() SQL.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measure.agg: percentile with agg_params.percentile value — explicit percentile support
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not natively supported — requires cube.measures[].type: number with SQL expression
Other Platform Notes: LookML: measure type: percentile + percentile property; Power BI: DAX PERCENTILE.EXC / STDEV functions

Description: Standalone calculated metric defined as an MDX expression operating on existing metrics — equivalent to a calculated member; supports mdx_aggregation_function for cross-model aggregation behavior; produces numerics from MDX calculations.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: MDX calculated metrics enable ratio, derived, and compound metrics using the full MDX function library (YTD, ParallelPeriod, NonEmptyCrossJoin, etc.) that SQL-only platforms cannot express; connects MDX-based BI tools (Excel, SSAS, Power BI pivot tables) to the semantic model.
Human Importance: High — MDX calculations extend the metric library beyond what SQL aggregations support; essential for Excel PivotTable users and OLAP-native BI tools
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents typically query via SQL or REST; MDX path is less common but available through AtScale's MDX interface for agent frameworks that speak XMLA
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not applicable — Snowflake SV uses SQL expressions in metrics[]
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No MDX support in Snowflake SV; all calculations must be SQL.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not applicable — UC MV uses SQL expressions in measures[]
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No MDX support in UC MV; all calculations must be SQL.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metric type: derived — SQL-based derived metrics (no MDX)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].type: number — SQL-based derived measure (no MDX)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: DAX calculated measures (closest — MDX-level expressiveness in a modern language)

Description: Inline arrays within a model definition that specify which metrics and dimension hierarchy levels to hide for a given audience — inverted model (hide what is NOT relevant rather than include what IS relevant).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Audience-specific perspectives reduce the semantic surface area visible to a team or agent context — a Finance agent sees only finance-relevant metrics; hidden items are invisible to both humans and agents without needing separate model files.
Human Importance: High — perspectives simplify field picker and report authoring for different business teams by hiding irrelevant metrics
Agentic Importance: High — agents operating in a perspective context reason over a smaller more relevant model; fewer irrelevant fields reduces misselection and improves query accuracy
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All SV dimensions/metrics always exposed; no perspective concept.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: All UC MV dimensions/measures always exposed.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — separate semantic_model definitions per team provide similar scoping
Other Platform Notes: LookML: explores as audience-specific facades; Power BI: perspectives; Cube: views[]

Description: Defines drillthrough query specifications within a model — specifies which metrics and detail attributes are available when a user drills through from an aggregated cell to underlying row data.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Governed drillthrough paths prevent agents and users from querying arbitrary raw columns when drilling through — ensures consistent, governed access to detail data at the row level.
Human Importance: High — drillthrough is a fundamental BI exploration interaction; governed paths ensure users reach the right detail columns
Agentic Importance: Medium — agents with investigative capability can use drillthrough definitions to navigate from aggregated anomalies to underlying rows
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No drillthrough concept in Snowflake SV.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No drillthrough concept in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt does not define drillthrough paths
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].drill_members[] — fields to expose on drill-down
Other Platform Notes: LookML: measure.drill_fields — closest equivalent; ThoughtSpot: drill anywhere is automatic without declaration

Description: Named pre-aggregated summary tables registered within a model — specifies which metrics and grouping attributes to summarize; AtScale automatically routes compatible queries to the most appropriate aggregate; supports in-memory caching via engine-memory option.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Transparent query acceleration via aggregate awareness — agents and users always query the semantic interface but compatible queries automatically hit pre-aggregated tables for sub-second performance.
Human Importance: High — transparent query routing to pre-aggregated tables means users get fast results without changing their queries or knowing which table was used
Agentic Importance: Low — agents benefit from faster responses but do not interact with aggregate definitions directly
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No semantic-layer-level aggregate routing in Snowflake SV; uses materialized views externally.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Experimental; less feature-rich than AtScale aggregate awareness (no partial match routing).
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[].exports[] materialize metric output; no in-model routing
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.pre_aggregations[] — most comparable aggregate awareness implementation
Other Platform Notes: LookML: aggregate_table in explores (closest equivalent); ThoughtSpot: aggregated_models

Description: Combines multiple models that share at least one common dimension into a single queryable semantic entity — enables cross-model metrics (metric_calc spanning multiple models) and cross-model user-defined aggregates.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Enables governed multi-fact analysis from a single semantic interface — agents can query measures from the Sales model and the Inventory model simultaneously without knowing the underlying join logic or data sources.
Human Importance: High — multi-fact models enable the cross-functional analysis (e.g. revenue + inventory) that executives most frequently request
Agentic Importance: High — composite models present a unified semantic surface to agents; without this construct agents must issue separate queries to different models and join results themselves
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV is a single-model artifact; cross-table metrics are supported via view-level derived metrics but not as a composite of separate models.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV is a single-model artifact; multi-model composition requires separate views or a warehouse-layer join view.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt MetricFlow joins across semantic models via entity graph
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not applicable — Cube manages cross-cube queries via views[] composing multiple cubes
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: multi-fact models via model_tables[] with multiple fact tables; LookML: multi-explore patterns

Description: Standalone row-level security definition using a lookup dataset that maps users/groups to permitted attribute values — scope controls which tables are filtered (related / fact / fact-only / all); secure_totals applies security to MDX subtotals; use_filter_key toggles between lookup-then-constrain and direct join approaches.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Declarative row-level security at the semantic layer enforces data scoping for all consumers (BI tools, API, MDX clients, agents) from a single governed definition — agents operating under user identities automatically see correctly scoped data.
Human Importance: Low — security is transparent to users; they see only permitted rows without knowing about RLS
Agentic Importance: High — semantic-layer-level RLS ensures agents operating under user identities never access out-of-scope rows; the most important governance property for multi-tenant agent deployments
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake row access policies provide RLS at the warehouse level; cannot be defined inside the semantic view.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported in UC MV YAML
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC row filters are UC catalog-level policies not embedded in the metric view.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt Semantic Layer access controlled by API credentials
Other Platform Notes: LookML: access_filter with user_attribute; Power BI: RLS with DAX filter expressions; ThoughtSpot: RLS defined separately in platform settings

Description: The predecessor to CREATE SEMANTIC VIEW — a pure YAML file stored in a Snowflake stage that served as the complete AI context document for Cortex Analyst; notable for having default_aggregation on measures (tells AI the default agg to use before the user specifies one) and a filters[] section with named reusable WHERE predicates.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: The legacy semantic_model.yaml was purpose-built as an AI artifact — every property was designed for LLM consumption rather than traditional BI; understanding its additional properties (default_aggregation, using_relationships) reveals what was lost in the transition to CREATE SEMANTIC VIEW.
Human Importance: N/A — this is a supplementary AI artifact format not a BI user interface element
Agentic Importance: High — default_aggregation and filters[] as named reusable predicates directly improve Cortex Analyst query accuracy
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Legacy YAML (semantic_model.yaml stored in Snowflake stage)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Purpose-built for LLM consumption; all properties are AI-facing. Con: Being superseded by CREATE SEMANTIC VIEW DDL. Con: stored in stage — not a first-class DB object.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: N/A
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: LSDL AI Instructions (conceptually similar — separate AI artifact alongside core model)

Property: Snowflake: legacy filters[] (named reusable WHERE predicates in AI context)
Description: Named filter predicates in the legacy semantic_model.yaml — each has name / synonyms / description / expr; Cortex Analyst can reference these named filters by name or description when resolving query intent rather than generating raw WHERE clauses.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Reusable named filters let the AI resolve phrases like 'completed orders' or 'enterprise customers' to validated, governed SQL predicates rather than hallucinating filter conditions — the most effective way to prevent invalid filter generation.
Human Importance: N/A — supplementary AI artifact
Agentic Importance: High — named filters with synonyms dramatically reduce the rate at which Cortex Analyst generates incorrect or invalid filter values
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Legacy format; partially superseded by Snowflake SV table-level filters[] (which serve the same purpose). Pro: Synonyms on filters extend the NL vocabulary beyond field synonyms.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: N/A
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: metrics[].filter with Jinja conditions — closest dbt equivalent (per-metric not reusable)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.segments[] — named reusable filter predicates (closest Cube equivalent)
Other Platform Notes: LookML: named filter fields (dimension type: filter); ThoughtSpot: model-level filters (no synonyms)

Property: Snowflake: default_aggregation on measures (legacy AI context property)
Description: Property in the legacy semantic_model.yaml that tells Cortex Analyst which aggregation to apply to a fact/measure when the user's query does not specify one — e.g. SUM / AVG / COUNT.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Without default_aggregation an AI must guess the correct aggregation; a wrong default (AVG instead of SUM for revenue) produces silently incorrect results on every query that does not explicitly specify the aggregation.
Human Importance: N/A — supplementary AI artifact
Agentic Importance: High — default aggregation is one of the highest-impact properties for agent correctness; an incorrect default produces wrong answers silently
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Legacy format property — not available in CREATE SEMANTIC VIEW YAML or DDL. The metrics[].expr in SV embeds the aggregation directly in SQL instead.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not supported (UC MV embeds aggregation in measures[].expr)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Same — aggregation is embedded in SQL expression; no separate default_aggregation field.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: measure.agg defines the canonical aggregation (explicit and required)
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.measures[].type defines the aggregation (explicit and required)
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: properties.aggregation on each column; all modern platforms embed agg in the expression

Property: Snowflake: using_relationships (multi-path join disambiguation for AI)
Description: Property on metrics in the legacy Cortex Analyst format that forces the AI to use a specific named relationship when multiple valid join paths exist between the same tables — prevents the AI from choosing an incorrect path.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: In multi-path schemas (e.g. fact connected to a date dimension via both order_date and ship_date foreign keys) the AI may choose the wrong relationship; using_relationships pins the metric to the correct path.
Human Importance: N/A — supplementary AI artifact
Agentic Importance: High — multi-path join ambiguity is a common source of incorrect agent query results; explicit disambiguation eliminates this class of error
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: using_relationships concept is preserved in the new DDL format. Con: YAML equivalent not documented as of early 2026.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no join-path disambiguation hint for metrics.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — MetricFlow entity graph prevents multi-path ambiguity by construction
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not applicable — Cube joins are always explicit in the schema
Other Platform Notes: LookML: always_join on explores forces specific joins; AtScale SML: relationships resolve via the model definition

Description: A separately managed store of verified Q&A pairs (question / SQL / verified_at / verified_by / use_as_onboarding_question) that Cortex Analyst uses for few-shot grounding — can be embedded in the SV DDL or managed via a dedicated API; accumulated verified queries can auto-optimize the semantic view definition (Dec 2025 preview).
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Verified examples are the most effective mechanism for improving agent accuracy on common business questions; a corpus of 30–50 verified queries can raise accuracy by 30%+ on similar questions; auto-optimization extracts filters and attributes from accumulated verified SQL to enrich the model.
Human Importance: High — onboarding questions surface as clickable suggested prompts in the Cortex Analyst UI; directly guides human exploration on first use
Agentic Importance: High — verified Q&A pairs serve as in-context few-shot examples for Cortex Analyst; the highest ROI investment for improving agent accuracy on known common questions
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Native (AI_VERIFIED_QUERIES in DDL / verified_queries[] in YAML / Verified Query Repository API)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Three management paths — inline DDL, YAML section, or external API. Pro: Dec 2025 optimization auto-enriches model from accumulated queries. Con: Verified SQL must reference logical names not physical columns.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent concept in UC MV; Genie learns from user feedback separately but verified queries are not embedded in the metric view definition.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[] — closest dbt equivalent (pre-defined queries not Q&A pairs)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: Verified Answers in LSDL (closest equivalent — trigger phrases + visual binding); LookML: Golden Queries via Conversational Analytics API

Description: Machine-readable JSON artifact generated at dbt compile time (target/semantic_manifest.json) containing the complete semantic layer definition — semantic_models, metrics, saved_queries, and project_configuration — consumed by the dbt MCP server, MetricFlow, and downstream tool integrations.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: The semantic_manifest is the authoritative runtime AI context document for the dbt ecosystem — every AI integration reads it rather than the raw YAML files; it is what makes the semantic layer portable across MCP clients, BI tools, and agent frameworks.
Human Importance: N/A — generated artifact not authored directly
Agentic Importance: High — all dbt AI integrations (MCP server, dbt Copilot, BI tool connectors) consume the manifest; its completeness directly determines agent accuracy
Snowflake SV Equivalent: Not directly applicable (Snowflake SV is a DB object not a file artifact)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV metadata is queried via SHOW SEMANTIC VIEWS and DDL introspection — no portable manifest file.
Databricks UC Equivalent: Not directly applicable (UC MV is a DB object not a file artifact)
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV metadata is queried via SHOW CREATE TABLE — no portable manifest file.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Native — generated by dbt compile for every project
Cube.dev Equivalent: Not applicable — Cube exposes metadata via /meta REST endpoint not a compiled file
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: TML files are the closest equivalent (exportable YAML representation of the semantic model)

Description: A filtered subset of the semantic model defined in LSDL (Logical Semantic Definition Language) — separate from the core TMDL model definition — that specifies which tables, columns, and measures are included in or excluded from Copilot and data agent queries; reduces AI reasoning surface area and improves accuracy.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Scoping the AI context to the most relevant subset of a large enterprise model is the single most effective technique for improving Copilot accuracy; a Finance AI schema that hides HR and Operations tables prevents field confusion and irrelevant query generation.
Human Importance: Medium — AI Data Schema is a developer-configured artifact; users experience the effect as more accurate Copilot responses
Agentic Importance: High — a well-scoped AI Data Schema is the highest-ROI investment for improving Copilot and data agent query accuracy in large Power BI models
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake SV access_modifier: private on facts/metrics provides similar scoping at the field level. No model-level inclusion/exclusion schema concept.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV exposes all dimensions/measures; no AI-specific schema scoping artifact.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — MetricFlow exposes all defined metrics to all consumers
Cube.dev Equivalent: cube.views[].cubes[].includes/excludes — closest equivalent for consumer-facing scope reduction
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: is_hidden on columns reduces the surface area; LookML: explore.fields whitelist/blacklist

Property: Power BI/Fabric: AI Instructions (LSDL freeform NL text)
Description: Freeform natural language text stored in LSDL alongside the semantic model — provides business logic, terminology definitions, routing rules, and analysis defaults to Copilot; examples include disambiguation rules ('for profitability use Contribution_Margin not Gross_Profit'), business calendar definitions, and default filter preferences.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: AI Instructions are the LSDL equivalent of ThoughtSpot's Data Model Instructions — global NL directives that shape agent behavior without changing the semantic model schema; enables nuanced business context that cannot be expressed as structured properties.
Human Importance: Low — invisible to users; experienced as more accurate Copilot responses
Agentic Importance: High — well-authored AI Instructions can prevent the most common class of semantic confusion in agent queries; most impactful free-text AI context mechanism in Power BI
Snowflake SV Equivalent: AI_SQL_GENERATION clause in CREATE SEMANTIC VIEW DDL (closest Snowflake equivalent)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Snowflake's AI_SQL_GENERATION is semantically equivalent — free-text injected into the Cortex Analyst generation prompt. Con: DDL-only; no YAML equivalent. Con: Single field not a rich instruction document.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no equivalent AI instruction artifact; descriptions and comments are passive context.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable
Other Platform Notes: ThoughtSpot: Data Model Instructions (closest equivalent); LookML: Conversational Analytics system instructions (API-level, not LSDL)

Description: Binding between natural language trigger phrases (up to 15 per answer) and a specific Power BI visual (with column, measure, and filter configuration) stored in LSDL; up to 250 verified answers per model; when Copilot semantically matches a trigger phrase the visual's structure guides DAX generation rather than free-form inference.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: The most structured form of verified Q&A pair across all platforms — not just a question+SQL pair but a question bound to a specific governed visual configuration; ensures agents produce the exact intended visualization for known common questions.
Human Importance: High — verified answers surface in the Copilot UI as suggested prompts and produce consistent governed responses to common questions
Agentic Importance: High — verified answers eliminate hallucination risk for the most frequently asked questions; agents produce the governed answer instead of generating a potentially incorrect free-form response
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake verified queries bind question to SQL not to a visual specification. Pro: SQL-level verification is more portable across consumers.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: No equivalent in UC MV.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[] — closest dbt equivalent (pre-defined queries not trigger-phrase bound)
Other Platform Notes: Snowflake: AI_VERIFIED_QUERIES (SQL-level); LookML: Golden Queries (API-level Looker URL); ThoughtSpot: no verified answer concept yet

Property: ThoughtSpot: Data Model Instructions (global NL directives)
Description: Global natural language directives stored within the ThoughtSpot model (accessible via the Instructions tab in the Data workspace) that apply to all Spotter queries on the model — define default date filter behavior, column preference disambiguation, conditional filtering rules, and automatic detail column inclusion; distinct from per-column ai_context.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Model-level NL instructions shape Spotter's global query generation behavior without changing any column definition — they are the highest-level AI context mechanism in ThoughtSpot, allowing model owners to encode business rules that apply across every user interaction.
Human Importance: Low — invisible to users; experienced as more intelligent Spotter responses
Agentic Importance: High — global instructions prevent the most common class of systematic Spotter errors (wrong date default, wrong column selection for ambiguous terms); more impactful per-instruction than per-column ai_context
Snowflake SV Equivalent: AI_SQL_GENERATION clause (closest Snowflake equivalent — view-level generation hint)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: Snowflake AI_SQL_GENERATION is a single free-text field not a rich multi-rule instruction document.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: Con: UC MV has no equivalent; column comments are passive not directive.
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: Not applicable — dbt Copilot context is implicit from descriptions
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: AI Instructions in LSDL (closest equivalent — structured freeform NL instruction document); LookML: Agent system instructions via Conversational Analytics API

Description: Verified NL→Explore-query pairs provided via the Looker Conversational Analytics API (not stored in LookML files) — each has natural_language_questions (array of phrasings) and a looker_query (model, explore, fields, filters, limit); recommended 30–50 pairs; distinct from LookML explore.query blocks which appear in the UI.
Agentic / Semantic Layer Importance: Few-shot grounding of Looker's Gemini-powered Conversational Analytics engine; verified examples that the AI uses to resolve ambiguous questions to specific field selections — most effective for questions where multiple plausible field combinations exist.
Human Importance: Medium — golden queries may surface as suggested starter questions; primarily a developer-configured accuracy enhancement
Agentic Importance: High — golden query pairs improve Conversational Analytics accuracy on known common questions; the 30–50 pair recommendation reflects the practical sweet spot for few-shot grounding without context bloat
Snowflake SV Equivalent: AI_VERIFIED_QUERIES (closest Snowflake equivalent — also Q&A pairs with verified metadata)
Snowflake Notes / Pros & Cons: Pro: Snowflake verified queries include verified_at timestamp, verified_by contact, and onboarding_question flag — more governance metadata than Looker's golden queries.
Databricks Notes / Pros & Cons: N/A
dbt MetricFlow Equivalent: saved_queries[] — pre-defined queries (not NL→query pairs)
Other Platform Notes: Power BI: Verified Answers in LSDL (most structured equivalent); ThoughtSpot: no equivalent yet; Snowflake: AI_VERIFIED_QUERIES

Property: Looker: Agent system instructions (Conversational Analytics API)
Description: YAML-formatted context document provided via the Looker Conversational Analytics API (published_context or inline_context) — includes system_instruction (NL persona/role), glossaries (term / definition / synonyms) and additional_descriptions (free-text domain rules); not stored in LookML files.
